proposals.title: Quantifying the Transitional Flow Regime on Stepped Chutes: Aeration, Velocity, and Energy Dissipation Characteristics
proposals.motivation: The design and safety assessment of stepped spillways rely on the established binary classification of nappe and skimming flow regimes. However, field and laboratory observations pre-2026 indicate a persistent transitional flow state with properties distinct from either classic regime. This gap in fundamental hydraulic understanding leads to inaccuracies in predicting key parameters like aeration, flow velocity, and energy dissipation, which are critical for cavitation protection and structural stability.
proposals.hypothesis: The transitional flow regime on steep stepped chutes exhibits a unique, quantifiable signature in its air-water flow properties (air concentration, bubble count rate, interfacial velocity) and energy dissipation mechanics, which cannot be accurately predicted by extrapolating models from the nappe or skimming flow regimes.
proposals.method: The research will employ detailed physical modelling in a large-scale laboratory flume with a stepped chute of fixed geometry (step height/length ratio typical of prototype dams). Using phase-detection intrusive probes (fibre-optic and conductivity probes), high-resolution measurements of air concentration, bubble count rate, and interfacial velocity will be systematically collected across a range of dimensionless discharges (dc/h) encompassing nappe, transition, and skimming flows. Data analysis will focus on identifying discontinuities and scaling laws in vertical profiles of these parameters. The energy dissipation will be assessed via detailed head loss calculations from upstream to downstream, correlating dissipation efficiency with the measured flow structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset and empirical criteria for defining the bounds and internal structure of the transitional flow regime. It will deliver improved predictive relationships for aeration and velocity in this regime, directly benefiting stepped spillway design. The findings will challenge the simplistic binary model, leading to more accurate and safer hydraulic engineering guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of transition flow is noted in literature, this study systematically quantifies its defining hydraulic properties, treating it not as an anomaly but as a distinct regime with its own scaling laws, directly challenging the adequacy of the two-regime framework.

proposals.title: Aeration, Pressure, and Energy Dissipation in Transition Flows on Stepped Chutes: A Criterion for Hydraulic Design
proposals.motivation: Stepped chutes are widely used for energy dissipation, with design guidelines well-established for fully-developed skimming and nappe flows. However, the transition flow regime, occurring at intermediate discharges, is poorly defined and presents unique challenges. It exhibits unstable, pulsating behavior with distinct aeration characteristics and potentially damaging dynamic pressure fluctuations on steps and sidewalls, posing risks to structural integrity and operational safety.
proposals.hypothesis: A dimensionless criterion based on the ratio of step roughness to flow depth (k_s/h) and the Reynolds number can robustly define the onset and bounds of the transition flow regime, correlating directly with measurable shifts in aeration efficiency, mean dynamic pressure on step faces, and energy dissipation rates.
proposals.method: The research will combine physical modelling and prototype data analysis. Systematic experiments on a large-scale stepped flume will measure air-water flow properties (void fraction, bubble count, velocity) using phase-detection probes, and dynamic pressures using miniature transducers. The data will be analyzed to identify thresholds where aeration and pressure patterns diverge from established regimes. Dimensional analysis and machine learning techniques (specifically, a Random Forest classifier) will be applied to historical and new datasets to develop a predictive criterion for transition flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical design guideline specifically for the transition flow regime, enabling engineers to predict aeration demands and sidewall pressure loads. It will refine the application range of existing stepped chute design manuals and improve the safety and reliability of spillway operations by addressing a previously ambiguous hydraulic condition.
proposals.novelty: While transition flow is acknowledged, no pre-2026 study has systematically linked its defining hydraulic parameters to quantifiable design variables for aeration and wall pressure. The application of machine learning to classify flow regime from multiphase properties in this context is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Discharge and Flow Regime from Air–Water Flow Measurements on Stepped Spillways
proposals.motivation: Current hydraulic engineering practice relies on direct measurement of discharge and velocity to characterize stepped chute flows, yet these measurements are difficult and costly on full-scale prototype structures. Air–water flow properties such as void fraction and bubble count rate profiles are comparatively easier to acquire using phase-detection intrusive probes, but no systematic inverse framework exists to reconstruct bulk flow parameters from these aeration signatures. Establishing such inverse methods would enable cost-effective field monitoring and improve real-time assessment of spillway performance and energy dissipation.
proposals.hypothesis: Void fraction and bubble count rate profiles measured across stepped chute flows contain sufficient information to uniquely infer the unit discharge and identify the flow regime (nappe, transition, or skimming), and a regularised inverse model coupling advection-diffusion theory with empirical bubble-size scaling can recover these quantities within 10% accuracy relative to direct measurements.
proposals.method: The approach combines physics-based forward modelling with Bayesian inversion. First, an extended advection-diffusion model for air concentration, parameterised by mean flow velocity, turbulent diffusivity, and bubble production rate, is calibrated against Chanson's existing laboratory datasets on stepped chutes. Second, a Tikhonov-regularised least-squares inversion is formulated to minimise the misfit between predicted and measured void fraction and bubble count profiles, solving simultaneously for discharge and diffusivity. Third, a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler quantifies posterior uncertainty on inferred discharge and classifies the flow regime via model selection among regime-specific forward models. The framework is validated against prototype data from Chanson's comparative physical-model versus prototype studies, testing generalisability across Reynolds number scales.
proposals.contribution: This work would deliver the first validated inverse framework that extracts discharge and flow regime directly from routinely measured air–water flow profiles on stepped spillways. It provides hydraulic engineers with a practical diagnostic tool for prototype monitoring where conventional discharge measurement is impractical. The uncertainty quantification component offers principled confidence bounds, advancing the field beyond deterministic empirical correlations.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of self-aerated flow are well established, the inverse problem—recovering bulk hydraulic parameters from aeration data—has not been formally posed or solved. Combining physics-constrained inversion with Bayesian regime classification is a non-obvious synthesis that bridges air–water flow physics and statistical inference in a way not previously attempted in stepped spillway hydraulics.

proposals.title: Reversed Aeration and Energy Dynamics in Upward-Stepped Channel Flows: A Dual-Configuration Investigation
proposals.motivation: Stepped spillways and chutes have been extensively studied for downward flows, where self-aeration and energy dissipation are well characterized. However, the inverse problem—flow ascending stepped channels under pressurized or pumped conditions—remains virtually unexplored despite its relevance to fish passage design, lock filling systems, and coastal overtopping of stepped revetments. Understanding how step geometry governs air entrainment and energy exchange in the ascending configuration would fill a fundamental gap in hydraulic engineering knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: When water ascends a stepped channel, the aeration mechanism reverses: air is entrained primarily at the step risers through recirculation vortices rather than at the pseudo-bottom as in descending flows, resulting in a qualitatively different void fraction distribution and a net energy input requirement that scales non-linearly with step height-to-critical-depth ratio.
proposals.method: Physical model experiments will be conducted in a tilting flume fitted with interchangeable step inserts (step heights 25–100 mm, slopes 1V:2H to 1V:4H) operated under both ascending (pump-driven) and descending gravity-driven conditions at matching unit discharges (0.05–0.20 m²/s). Dual-tip conductivity probes will measure local void fraction, bubble frequency, and interfacial velocity profiles at identical dimensionless positions along both configurations. High-speed imaging (2000 fps) will capture cavity dynamics at the step risers to identify the entrainment mechanism in the ascending case. Energy budget analysis will compare residual energy head between ascending and descending flows using total pressure and velocity head measurements, with dimensional analysis yielding predictive relationships for the energy dissipation ratio as a function of step Reynolds number and relative step height.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic dataset of air-water flow properties in ascending stepped channels, enabling direct comparison with the extensive descending-flow literature. The resulting empirical correlations for void fraction profiles and energy head loss in the ascending configuration will inform the design of stepped structures where upward flow occurs, such as labyrinth weir aprons, lock chamber filling systems, and stepped coastal defenses subjected to wave run-up.
proposals.novelty: While decades of research have characterized self-aeration in descending stepped flows, no study has formally treated the ascending case as the dual hydraulic problem. By isolating the effect of gravity reversal relative to flow direction on aeration onset and cavity dynamics, this work reveals whether existing self-aeration scaling laws can be generalized or require fundamentally new formulations.

proposals.title: A Perturbation Framework for Unsteady Correction Factors in Rapidly-Varying Open Channel Flows: Application to Stepped Chutes and Hydraulic Structures
proposals.motivation: Practical hydraulic design of chutes, spillways, and channels relies heavily on steady, uniform flow equations, despite the prevalence of unsteady, rapidly-varying flows like flood waves and surges. This disconnect introduces unknown errors in critical design parameters such as depth, velocity, and energy dissipation. A tractable method to quantify and correct these errors is needed to improve the safety and efficiency of hydraulic structures without resorting to computationally intensive unsteady simulations for every design scenario.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic perturbation expansion of the Saint-Venant equations around a steady-state baseline, using dimensionless unsteadiness indicators, will yield closed-form correction factors that accurately predict flow properties in unsteady, rapidly-varying conditions relevant to stepped chutes and steep spillways.
proposals.method: The method develops a hierarchical perturbation framework by non-dimensionalizing the Saint-Venant equations and expanding key variables (depth, velocity) around the steady uniform solution. Dimensionless parameters governing unsteadiness (e.g., normalized hydrograph rise rate, ∂F/∂x) will be identified. First- and second-order correction terms will be derived analytically. The framework will be validated against pre-2026 high-resolution CFD datasets (e.g., dam-break, surge propagation) and new laboratory experiments in a stepped flume, instrumented with dual-tip phase detection probes and ADV to measure air-water flow properties and velocities under controlled unsteady inflows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide practicing engineers with a practical, equation-based tool to assess the limits of steady-state assumptions and apply corrective factors for unsteady effects. It will directly extend Professor Chanson's research on energy and momentum in self-aerated flows by providing a formal analytical link between steady design equations and observed unsteady prototype behavior, enhancing predictive accuracy for energy dissipation on stepped spillways.
proposals.novelty: While perturbation methods are known in fluid mechanics, their structured application to derive *practical correction factors* for the Saint-Venant equations in the context of aerated, rapidly-varying open channel flows represents a novel bridge between theoretical hydraulics and routine engineering design.

proposals.title: A Chapman-Enskog-like expansion for unsteady open channel flows: Deriving systematic corrections to the Saint-Venant equations
proposals.motivation: Standard hydraulic models for unsteady flows, like the Saint-Venant equations, rely on steady-state friction laws (e.g., Manning) and neglect non-hydrostatic and turbulent memory effects. This leads to significant errors in predicting extreme events like flash floods and dam-break waves, where rapid temporal accelerations dominate. A systematic framework to derive and validate higher-order corrections is critically needed to improve predictive accuracy in hydraulic engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The governing equations for unsteady, free-surface flow can be reformulated using a small dimensionless parameter (ε) representing the ratio of local/temporal acceleration to gravitational forcing. A perturbation expansion in ε will systematically yield zeroth-order (steady uniform), first-order (unsteady friction, dispersion), and higher-order terms capturing non-equilibrium turbulent and non-hydrostatic effects absent from standard models.
proposals.method: The expansion parameter ε will be formally defined from the dimensionless momentum equation. The depth-averaged Saint-Venant system will be rewritten in terms of ε. A Chapman-Enskog-inspired perturbation will be applied: the flow variables (depth, velocity) are expressed as an asymptotic series. Zeroth-order solves for steady, uniform flow. First and second-order problems will be solved analytically and numerically to derive explicit correction terms for friction and dispersion. The framework will be validated against existing physical model data from steep, stepped chutes and dam-break wave experiments, comparing predictions of wave front celerity and energy dissipation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, physics-based method to derive unsteady friction laws and dispersive terms directly from the flow equations, moving beyond empirical calibrations. It will deliver explicit dimensionless criteria (e.g., critical values of ε) to guide model selection, from simple steady models to complex unsteady simulations, for practicing engineers.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and mathematical analogy between the Chapman-Enskog expansion in kinetic theory and a perturbation expansion for open channel hydraulics is novel. It provides a first-principles pathway to unsteady corrections, unlike prior ad-hoc or empirically tuned models for unsteady friction.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-ML Framework for Scale-Corrected Unsteady Flow Predictions in Steep, Self-Aerated Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: Standard steady-uniform flow models are routinely applied to design hydraulic structures like stepped spillways, but they fail to capture the complex, rapidly-varying unsteady aerated flows observed in prototypes, leading to errors in energy dissipation and flow depth estimates. Furthermore, physical scale models, while essential, introduce scale effects in air-water turbulence and wave dynamics that distort predictions when extrapolated to field scale. A unified method to correct steady-state model bias while accounting for these scale distortions is critically needed for reliable infrastructure design and operation.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network can learn a spatiotemporally varying correction function that maps steady-state flow model outputs to accurate unsteady flow quantities, and this function can inherently encapsulate the systematic distortions caused by physical scale effects, creating a single predictive tool valid from laboratory to prototype scales.
proposals.method: We will generate paired datasets from detailed laboratory experiments (small and large flumes), a pilot outdoor channel, and prototype stepped spillway measurements, capturing high-fidelity air-water velocity, depth, and turbulence data under unsteady flow conditions. Corresponding steady-state model predictions (e.g., Darcy-Weisbach, Chanson's energy considerations) will be computed for each dataset. A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, adept at spatial feature extraction, will be trained to regress the discrepancy between the steady prediction and the measured unsteady state. The network's input will be the spatial field of steady-state variables (velocity, pressure gradient) plus dimensionless scale parameters (Reynolds, Weber, Froude numbers) to explicitly inform the scale correction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel hybrid modelling framework that fuses classical hydraulic theory with data-driven correction, directly applicable to the design of stepped chutes and other rapidly-varied flows. It will provide a quantitative map of flow regimes where steady-state assumptions are most erroneous and deliver a practical tool that eliminates the need for separate, often subjective, scale effect analyses in physical modelling.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely integrates the correction of steady-flow model bias with the compensation for physical scale effects into a single, learned function, moving beyond traditional separate analyses of unsteady flow and scale modelling. It applies state-of-the-art CNNs, common in image analysis, to the spatial correction of hydraulic flow fields, a novel application in open channel hydraulics.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Turbulence and Aeration Dynamics in Stepped Chute Flows: A Direct Numerical Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: One-dimensional (1D) open channel flow models, while practical, fundamentally oversimplify the complex, three-dimensional (3D) nature of self-aerated flows over stepped spillways. These simplifications obscure critical local phenomena—such as vortex generation at step edges, lateral air entrainment patterns, and 3D energy dissipation mechanisms—which are central to accurate hydraulic design and safety assessment. A fully 3D, turbulence-resolving investigation is needed to challenge the 1D paradigm and provide a physically rigorous foundation for flow properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fully three-dimensional, turbulence-resolving simulations will reveal dominant secondary currents and coherent structures that fundamentally control air-water transfer and energy dissipation rates in stepped chute flows, demonstrating significant deviations from predictions based on one-dimensional flow assumptions.
proposals.method: The research will employ Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) and well-resolved Large Eddy Simulation (LES) using the open-source code OpenFOAM (pre-2026 v8 or v9). The Volume of Fluid (VOF) method will model the air-water interface, with specific focus on the interFoam solver. Simulations will replicate established laboratory-scale stepped chute geometries from Chanson's prior work. Analysis will utilize the finite volume method on high-resolution, structured grids to resolve the Kolmogorov scale (DNS) or the inertial subrange (LES), with post-processing to quantify 3D vorticity, turbulent kinetic energy budgets, and interfacial area density.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully 3D, turbulence-resolved dataset for stepped chute flows, quantifying the error inherent in 1D models for key parameters like velocity, air concentration, and energy dissipation. It will deliver new physical insight into the link between 3D turbulent structures and bulk flow properties, offering a benchmark for improving future reduced-order models and stepped spillway design guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While 3D RANS simulations exist, applying DNS/LES to resolve all turbulent scales in a free-surface, aerated flow on a stepped chute is novel. This approach moves beyond time-averaged statistics to analyze the instantaneous, 3D mechanisms driving air entrainment and energy loss, a perspective absent from prior physical and numerical modelling.

proposals.title: Field-Scale Validation and Calibration of Non-Newtonian Rheological Models for Steep, Self-Aerated Open Channel Flows
proposals.motivation: Predicting debris flows, hyperconcentrated sediment currents, and mud flows in steep channels is critical for hazard assessment, yet existing models rely heavily on laboratory-derived rheological parameters (e.g., Bingham, Herschel-Bulkley). A significant gap exists between these small-scale measurements and the complex, often aerated, conditions observed in real-world, large-scale flows on steep chutes and spillways. This work addresses the urgent need for field-calibrated constitutive relationships to improve the accuracy of engineering designs for debris flow corridors and muddy estuarine channels.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic comparison of Newtonian and non-Newtonian model predictions against comprehensive field datasets will reveal specific flow regimes (defined by sediment concentration, shear rate, and aeration) where Newtonian assumptions fail, and will yield optimal, field-validated rheological parameterizations for each regime.
proposals.method: The research will compile and analyze existing large-scale field measurement datasets of velocity profiles, depth, and sediment concentration from documented debris flow and hyperconcentrated flow events. For each dataset, predictions from Newtonian, Bingham plastic, Herschel-Bulkley, and power-law models will be generated using a 1D momentum integral approach, extended to account for air-water mixture density effects relevant to self-aerated flows on steep chutes. A discrepancy analysis framework, quantifying errors in predicted vs. observed velocity and depth, will be applied to diagnose model performance across different Froude numbers and volumetric sediment concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a diagnostic methodology for selecting and calibrating rheological models based on observable field conditions. It will deliver a set of field-validated constitutive relationships and practical guidelines for engineers, directly improving the predictive accuracy of models used for the design of chutes, spillways, and hazard mitigation structures in non-Newtonian flow environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing Hubert Chanson's expertise in self-aeration and energy dissipation in steep, stepped chutes with non-Newtonian rheology, creating a validation framework that explicitly accounts for air-water-sediment interactions at field scale, which is largely absent from pre-2026 laboratory-focused studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Scale Effects in Stepped Spillway Modelling: A Rigorous Validation of Physical and Numerical Predictions Against Prototype Air-Water Flow Data
proposals.motivation: The design of high-velocity hydraulic structures like stepped spillways relies heavily on physical and numerical modelling. However, significant scale effects, particularly concerning air entrainment, bubble dynamics, and energy dissipation, persist and are not fully quantified. A systematic, multi-parameter validation against high-quality prototype data is urgently needed to establish the limits of applicability and improve predictive confidence for engineers.
proposals.hypothesis: That a rigorous, multi-fidelity validation protocol, comparing both Froude-scaled physical models and advanced numerical simulations (VOF-based RANS) against comprehensive prototype datasets, will reveal predictable, quantifiable biases in key air-water flow properties (velocity, aeration, energy dissipation) that can be corrected via empirical scaling relationships.
proposals.method: The research will utilize existing, high-resolution prototype datasets from large stepped chutes (e.g., Gold Creek Dam, Paradise Dam). Physical model data will be generated in a large laboratory flume under Froude similitude. Numerical modelling will employ a Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method within a RANS framework with a k-ε turbulence closure, as implemented in OpenFOAM or similar pre-2026 solvers. The validation will be a point-by-point statistical comparison (e.g., RMSE, bias analysis) of key parameters: depth-averaged velocity, air concentration profiles, and residual energy at the chute toe.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated framework for stepped chute model assessment, directly quantifying scale effects for critical design parameters. It will provide concrete guidance on when numerical models can supplement or replace physical models, and deliver empirically-adjusted scaling laws to improve the extrapolation of laboratory results to prototype scales.
proposals.novelty: While scale effects are known, this proposal's novelty lies in its systematic, tripartite (prototype, physical model, CFD) validation focused on air-water flow energetics, moving beyond qualitative comparison to a rigorous statistical quantification of model error and bias for engineering design.

proposals.title: A VOF-LES Framework for Predicting Free-Surface Dynamics and Aeration Onset in Steep Stepped Chutes
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of free-surface position and self-aeration onset in high-velocity chute flows remains a critical challenge in hydraulic engineering, impacting energy dissipation estimates and structural design. While physical models provide insights, they are constrained by scale effects, particularly for air-water interactions. A robust, validated computational framework could bridge the gap between laboratory-scale observations and prototype-scale predictions for complex stepped spillways.
proposals.hypothesis: A coupled Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) and Large Eddy Simulation (LES) model, incorporating a turbulence-induced air entrainment criterion, can accurately predict the unknown free-surface profile and the inception point of self-aeration on steep stepped chutes, matching detailed prototype velocity and aeration data.
proposals.method: The research will implement a VOF method within the OpenFOAM framework to capture the sharp air-water interface. The interFoam solver will be coupled with a Wall-Adapting Local Eddy-viscosity (WALE) LES model to resolve turbulence. A key model component is the implementation of a pre-2026 air entrainment inception criterion (e.g., based on local turbulent kinetic energy exceeding surface tension forces) to trigger air phase introduction. Simulations will be validated against Professor Chanson's own detailed prototype velocity and void fraction measurements from steep stepped chutes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a high-fidelity, open-source CFD methodology specifically tuned for predicting air-water flows on hydraulic structures. It will provide new insights into the mechanistic link between near-surface turbulence and aeration onset, moving beyond empirical correlations. The validated model will serve as a tool for evaluating scale effects in physical modelling of stepped chutes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of a VOF-LES model with a physics-based air entrainment trigger for hydraulic engineering flows, and its rigorous validation against unique, full-scale prototype data—a step beyond common laboratory-scale CFD validations in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Physics-constrained deep learning for full-depth discharge estimation from drone-based surface velocimetry in steep, aerated chute flows
proposals.motivation: Remote sensing techniques like LSPIV provide crucial non-intrusive surface velocity data in hazardous, high-energy flows such as steep chutes and spillways, where traditional gauging is dangerous or impossible. However, existing discharge estimation relies on empirical surface coefficients, which are highly site-specific and unreliable in aerated, rapidly varied flows, leading to large errors. A robust, physics-based method to infer depth-averaged velocity and discharge from surface observations is urgently needed for hydraulic engineering and dam safety.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding the Saint-Venant equations and known principles of self-aerated flow velocity profiles as hard constraints within a neural network architecture will enable accurate, generalizable inference of depth-averaged velocity and total discharge from surface velocity fields and periodic bathymetry, significantly outperforming empirical coefficient methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) that ingests drone LSPIV surface velocity fields and Structure-from-Motion/SfM-MVS-derived bathymetry. The network's loss function will combine data fidelity terms with physics residuals from the 2D shallow water equations (mass and momentum conservation). A separate network branch will learn the site-specific, non-logarithmic velocity profile shape characteristic of aerated flows, informed by Chanson's prior work on air-water flow properties. The system will be validated against synchronized ADCP and phase-detection probe measurements in controlled laboratory stepped chutes and a prototype spillway.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, operational framework for accurate, non-intrusive discharge estimation in high-velocity, aerated open channels, directly applicable to dam spillways and steep chutes. It will advance the fundamental understanding of the relationship between surface and depth-averaged velocity in complex, aerated flows by moving beyond empirical coefficients to a physics-guided, data-driven model.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of remote sensing with PINNs for hydraulic discharge estimation is nascent; applying this specifically to the challenging domain of steep, self-aerated flows—where velocity profiles deviate strongly from standard laws—and explicitly incorporating prior knowledge of air-water flow properties represents a significant, non-obvious advancement.

proposals.title: Reach-Scale Turbulence Mapping in Stepped and Smooth Chutes via Drone-Based Large-Scale Particle Image Velocimetry
proposals.motivation: Prototype-scale validation of hydraulic models for energy dissipation and air-water flow dynamics remains a major challenge in environmental hydraulics. Existing field velocimetry (e.g., LSPIV) provides only mean surface velocities, while laboratory PIV is confined to small-scale flumes, creating a critical scale gap for turbulence characterization in natural and engineered channels like stepped spillways. A method to directly measure two-dimensional turbulent flow fields at the reach scale is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-frequency aerial imagery of natural surface tracers (foam, bubbles) or seeded particles, processed with advanced PIV algorithms, can resolve instantaneous velocity fields and key turbulence statistics (e.g., turbulent kinetic energy, Reynolds stresses) in prototype open-channel flows.
proposals.method: The research will deploy drones equipped with high-frame-rate cameras over prototype stepped chutes and smooth inverts. Image sequences of naturally occurring or artificially seeded surface tracers will be processed using open-source PIV algorithms (e.g., OpenPIV, PIVlab), adapted for large-scale, georectified imagery. The processing chain will include image pre-filtering, multi-pass cross-correlation with window deformation, and validation to compute instantaneous 2D velocity vectors. Time-series analysis of these fields will yield turbulence metrics and permit identification of coherent structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first field-scale, two-dimensional turbulence maps for prototype chute flows, directly validating RANS and LES models. It will provide novel insights into the scaling of energy dissipation and aeration processes from laboratory to prototype, with direct applications for sediment transport and ecological habitat assessment in high-velocity flows.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of drone remote sensing with laboratory-grade PIV processing to extract turbulence statistics is novel; prior remote sensing focused on bulk velocity, and prior PIV was lab-bound. This enables non-intrusive, spatially extensive turbulence measurement at the prototype scale.

proposals.title: Non-intrusive remote sensing of air-water flow properties on stepped chutes: A validation study using drone-based PIV and satellite imagery
proposals.motivation: Direct, intrusive measurement of high-velocity, self-aerated flows on steep chutes and spillways is challenging, hazardous, and can alter the flow physics. Remote sensing techniques, such as drone-based imaging and satellite-derived surface velocity, offer a non-intrusive alternative but have not been rigorously validated for complex, aerated, and highly turbulent flows typical of energy dissipation structures. This research addresses the need for reliable, scalable methods to measure prototype flow properties for validation of physical models and design.
proposals.hypothesis: Surface velocity fields and characteristic length scales (e.g., white water region extent, roller dimensions) in self-aerated stepped chute flows can be accurately quantified using particle image velocimetry (PIV) applied to drone video and satellite-based optical flow algorithms, with results comparable to established intrusive methods.
proposals.method: The study will combine field and laboratory work. At a prototype stepped spillway, synchronized measurements will be taken using drone-based video (4K, 60fps) and established intrusive techniques (e.g., dual-tip phase detection probes for velocity and aeration). Drone footage will be processed using open-source PIV software (e.g., PIVlab, OpenPIV) to derive surface velocity fields. Concurrently, high-resolution satellite imagery (e.g., PlanetScope, Sentinel-2) will be analyzed using the Farneback dense optical flow algorithm to estimate surface velocities at a larger scale. Laboratory experiments on a scaled physical model will provide controlled validation of the image processing techniques against detailed phase-detection probe data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, non-intrusive methodology for measuring key hydraulic parameters in prototype aerated flows, directly supporting the calibration and scaling of physical models. It will yield new datasets linking remotely sensed surface signatures to underlying air-water flow properties (velocity, aeration, turbulence), advancing the remote monitoring of hydraulic structures.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing is used in river hydrology, its application to highly aerated, stepped chute flows—where the air-water interface is violently agitated and optical properties differ from clear water—is novel. The direct cross-validation with intrusive phase-detection probe data in both field and laboratory settings represents a significant step beyond current state-of-the-art.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Channel Bed Topography from PIV-Measured Turbulence Fields in Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: Direct measurement of channel bathymetry in high-velocity, aerated flows, such as on stepped spillways, is often infeasible or intrusive, limiting accurate hydraulic modeling and infrastructure assessment. Existing inverse methods rely on sparse surface data, providing poor resolution for complex bedforms. A method to deduce geometry from detailed flow observations would enable non-intrusive monitoring of erosion, scour, and structural integrity in hydraulic structures.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially dense velocity and Reynolds stress fields obtained via Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) contain unique, high-dimensional signatures of bed-generated turbulence and secondary currents, which can be used as robust constraints to accurately reconstruct the underlying channel cross-section geometry through an inverse numerical framework.
proposals.method: High-resolution PIV will be deployed in a laboratory stepped chute to capture instantaneous 2D velocity fields and derived turbulence properties (Reynolds stresses, TKE) in self-aerated flows. These fields will serve as the observational input for an inverse solver built on a 2D Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) model with a k-ε or k-ω turbulence closure. The inverse problem will be solved using an adjoint-based optimization or a Bayesian inference algorithm (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) to iteratively adjust a parameterized bed geometry until the simulated flow fields match the PIV observations, minimizing a cost function based on velocity and turbulence residuals.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, non-intrusive bathymetry surveying technique specifically validated for turbulent, aerated flows relevant to dam safety and river engineering. It will advance fundamental understanding of the causal links between bed topography and the resulting turbulence structures in high-velocity open channels. The integrated PIV-inverse framework will provide a new tool for hydraulic engineers to assess channel geometry where direct measurement is impossible.
proposals.novelty: The approach fuses high-resolution, planar PIV turbulence data—a rich but previously untapped constraint—with inverse hydraulic modeling, moving beyond traditional methods that use only surface velocity or water level. It explicitly leverages coherent turbulence patterns as diagnostic features for geometry reconstruction, a concept not fully explored in pre-2026 hydraulic inverse problems.

proposals.title: High-Resolution PIV Characterization of Turbulent Coherent Structures and Momentum Flux in Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: The complex air-water turbulence on stepped spillways is critical for energy dissipation and flow resistance, yet its underlying coherent structures remain poorly quantified. Existing models for momentum and energy transfer rely on bulk parameters, lacking the detailed spatial-temporal data needed to validate and refine turbulence closures for these highly aerated, free-surface flows.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced PIV techniques will reveal distinct, quantifiable turbulent coherent structures (e.g., vortices, sweeps, ejections) within the aerated flow region, and their spatial organization directly governs the local momentum flux and energy dissipation rates.
proposals.method: A laboratory-scale stepped chute will be instrumented with a high-speed, planar PIV system using laser sheet illumination and neutrally buoyant, refractive-index-matched seeding particles suitable for the air-water mixture. Time-resolved velocity vector fields will be acquired in streamwise-vertical planes. Data will be processed using cross-correlation algorithms (e.g., iterative window deformation) and analyzed via Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) and quadrant analysis to identify and characterize dominant turbulent structures and momentum fluxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially-resolved measurements of turbulent coherent structures in aerated stepped chute flows, linking structure dynamics to bulk energy dissipation. It will deliver a validated dataset for improving RANS and LES turbulence models in hydraulic engineering, specifically for flows with strong aeration and surface discontinuities.
proposals.novelty: While PIV is established in clear-water hydraulics, its rigorous application to quantify turbulence within the optically challenging, two-phase flow region of a stepped chute is novel. The explicit focus on connecting identified structures to momentum flux, a key concept in Chanson's recent work, represents a significant advance beyond time-averaged velocity and aeration measurements.

proposals.title: Scaling Open-Channel Hydraulics to the Microfluidic Regime: A Study of Air-Water Flow Dynamics and Energy Dissipation in Capillary-Dominated Stepped Chutes
proposals.motivation: Microfluidic systems for chemical and biological analysis increasingly require precise control of multiphase flows, yet their design often lacks the rigorous hydraulic principles used in large-scale open channels. Prior to 2026, the scaling of self-aeration, energy dissipation, and flow resistance from macro-scale chutes, where gravity dominates, to micro-scale channels, where capillary and viscous forces prevail, remains an unexplored frontier. This research bridges the gap between traditional hydraulic engineering and lab-on-a-chip technology.
proposals.hypothesis: The fundamental principles governing air-water flow structure, momentum transfer, and energy dissipation in stepped chutes can be scaled down to microfluidic open channels by redefining the governing dimensionless numbers (e.g., replacing the Froude number with the Weber and capillary numbers) and that a distinct, quantifiable flow regime transition linked to surface tension will be observed.
proposals.method: The research will employ precision micro-fabrication to create scaled stepped chutes with characteristic dimensions from 50 to 500 µm. High-speed micro-Particle Image Velocimetry (µPIV) and laser-induced fluorescence will be used to measure velocity fields and air-water interface dynamics. The experimental protocol will systematically vary flow rate, step geometry, and fluid properties (surface tension, viscosity) to generate datasets. Data analysis will focus on deriving new scaling relationships by comparing measured parameters (air concentration, velocity profiles, energy loss) with established macro-scale correlations from Chanson's work, modified with capillary (Ca) and Weber (We) numbers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new theoretical framework for 'capillary hydraulics,' providing predictive models for flow resistance and phase distribution in micro-open channels. It will deliver practical design guidelines for microfluidic devices requiring efficient mixing, aeration, or controlled dissipation, directly impacting chemical synthesis and biomedical diagnostics. The findings will also offer a novel perspective on the fundamental limits of hydraulic scaling.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics extensively studies droplet and plug flows, applying the specific air-water flow and stepped chute energy dissipation concepts from large-scale hydraulics to open microchannels is unprecedented. The explicit investigation of self-aeration dynamics and momentum considerations at a scale where surface tension is the primary body force represents a significant conceptual leap.

proposals.title: A Coupled Adjoint-CFD Inversion Framework for Simultaneous Reconstruction of Channel Geometry and Free-Surface Profile in Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: In hydraulic engineering and river hydraulics, accurately determining channel geometry is critical for assessing flow capacity, energy dissipation, and scour risk. Traditional methods often rely on bathymetric surveys or simplified inverse models that assume a known free-surface, which fails in complex, aerated flows like those over stepped spillways where the air-water interface is dynamic and unknown. A method to jointly infer the wetted boundary and free-surface from sparse surface observations would transform the characterization of existing hydraulic structures and natural channels.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding a multiphase Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) solver as the forward model within an adjoint-based optimization framework can simultaneously and physically reconstruct both the unknown channel bed geometry and the free-surface profile from limited surface velocity and water level data, even in complex, self-aerated flow regimes.
proposals.method: The forward model will be a 3D unsteady Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (URANS) solver using a Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method for air-water interface capture. This CFD model will be embedded within an iterative inversion scheme using a continuous adjoint method to compute gradients of a cost function (mismatch between simulated and observed surface velocities/levels) with respect to the bed geometry and free-surface boundary conditions. The geometry will be parameterized using B-splines or a discrete mesh deformation technique. Validation will use synthetic data from known stepped chute CFD simulations and laboratory data from physical models of steep, aerated chutes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-based inversion tool that moves beyond simplistic hydraulic assumptions, directly applicable to prototype stepped spillway analysis and irregular river channels. It will provide a rigorous framework for fusing modern remote sensing (e.g., UAV-based surface velocimetry) with high-fidelity flow physics to deduce submerged topography, advancing the inverse modelling paradigm in hydraulics.
proposals.novelty: The simultaneous inversion for both the solid boundary and the free-surface within a full multiphase CFD framework is a significant departure from existing methods, which typically treat one as known. This explicitly addresses the coupled nature of the problem in aerated, supercritical flows central to Hubert Chanson's research.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Stepped Chute Geometry from Surface Flow Signatures: A Physical Modelling and Momentum-Based Approach
proposals.motivation: Accurate knowledge of channel geometry is critical for predicting flow resistance, energy dissipation, and aeration in steep hydraulic structures like spillways. However, geometry can be unknown due to sedimentation, erosion, or lack of as-built records. Current methods rely on direct surveying, which is often intrusive or impossible during operation. An inverse method using readily measurable surface parameters would enable non-invasive assessment and monitoring of critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The momentum integral and specific energy relationships governing self-aerated flows over rough inverts contain sufficient information to allow the unique reconstruction of the underlying channel cross-sectional geometry when constrained by surface velocity and water level measurements.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical modelling and analytical approach. Laboratory experiments on a variable-geometry stepped chute will provide controlled datasets of surface velocity (via LSPIV) and air-water flow depths for known geometries. The inverse problem will be formulated using the one-dimensional momentum equation for air-water mixtures, incorporating pre-2026 friction and aeration models from Chanson's work. A gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt) will be developed to iteratively adjust a parameterized bed profile until the predicted surface signatures match the measurements, minimizing a defined cost function.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-intrusive methodology for inferring channel bed geometry, directly applicable to the condition assessment of existing spillways and chutes. It will advance the fundamental understanding of the sensitivity of surface flow features to bed geometry in highly turbulent, aerated flows, providing a new diagnostic tool for hydraulic engineers.
proposals.novelty: While inverse problems are known in hydrology for catchment models, applying them to reconstruct the detailed geometry of a high-velocity, aerated chute from surface flow data is unexplored. The method uniquely integrates Chanson's established momentum-energy frameworks for self-aerated flows into an inverse formulation.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Hydraulic Resistance in Closed Conduits from Coupled Sediment Bed and Air Pocket Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In stormwater and wastewater force mains, the co-existence of sediment deposits and entrapped air pockets significantly alters flow resistance, impacting system capacity and energy dissipation. Current practice lacks methods to diagnose the composite resistance (grain, form, and air-induced losses) without intrusive flow measurements. A unified inverse framework using observable two-phase signatures would provide a practical diagnostic tool for asset management.
proposals.hypothesis: The complementary sensitivities of sediment bedform geometry and air pocket dynamics to different components of flow resistance enable their simultaneous inversion within a unified dynamic similarity framework, allowing for the robust decomposition of total resistance in closed conduits.
proposals.method: The methodology formulates two inverse problems: one for sediment transport (bedform wavelength/amplitude vs. bed shear stress) and one for de-aeration (air pocket length/migration vs. pressure gradient). These are coupled within an ensemble-based inversion scheme (e.g., Ensemble Kalman Filter) to assimilate data from laboratory flume-conduit experiments and field instruments. Validation will use controlled experiments in a stepped chute-conduit facility, leveraging detailed air-water flow measurements, and field data from instrumented sewer networks.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel diagnostic tool to infer composite hydraulic resistance from routine observations, eliminating the need for dedicated flow metering. It will advance the fundamental understanding of momentum and energy dissipation in complex, multi-phase free-surface and pressurized flows in closed conduits.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in exploiting the complementary data streams of two interacting but traditionally separate phenomena (sediment transport and air-water flow) within a single inverse framework to decompose flow resistance, a problem not addressed in prior inverse modeling or air-water flow research.

proposals.title: Inversion of Bedform Morphology for Flow Resistance Estimation in Stepped Chutes and Self-Aerated Flows
proposals.motivation: In hydraulic engineering, predicting flow resistance is critical for designing spillways and assessing energy dissipation. Traditional methods rely on direct flow measurements or empirical formulae, which can be difficult or impossible to obtain in prototype-scale, aerated flows over complex topography. A method to infer resistance from readily observable bedform patterns would provide a valuable diagnostic tool for both physical models and field observations.
proposals.hypothesis: The equilibrium geometry of sediment bedforms (e.g., step-pool sequences, antidunes) in a mobile bed is a direct signature of the flow's energy and momentum conditions, allowing for the back-calculation of an effective Darcy-Weisbach friction factor (f) or Manning's n.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical modelling and analytical approach. First, controlled experiments will be conducted in a steep, stepped flume with a mobile sediment bed, measuring detailed air-water flow properties (velocity, concentration) and resultant bedform geometry. The inversion will use a momentum integral analysis, incorporating pre-2026 sediment transport and bedform stability theories (e.g., Kennedy, van Rijn). The core algorithm will iteratively solve the governing equations (continuity, momentum, sediment transport) to find the friction coefficient that yields the observed equilibrium bed configuration for the measured bulk flow conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, morphology-based technique for estimating flow resistance in high-velocity, aerated environments. It will provide a new validation metric for stepped chute hydraulics and contribute to the fundamental understanding of the feedback between self-aeration, energy dissipation, and sediment transport dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While sediment transport predicts bedforms from known flow, this inverts the causality. It uniquely applies this inversion to the complex, highly aerated flows characteristic of Professor Chanson's research, linking bed morphology directly to air-water flow resistance parameters.

proposals.title: A Regime-Diagram Framework for Air-Water Flow Scaling: A Combustion-Inspired Analogy for Stepped Chutes and Self-Aerated Flows
proposals.motivation: Physical modelling of air entrainment in hydraulic structures like stepped chutes relies on Froude similitude, but significant scale effects persist due to neglected viscous and surface tension forces. These effects compromise the extrapolation of air concentration and energy dissipation data from laboratory to prototype scales. A systematic, physics-based framework to predict and correct for these scale effects is critically needed for reliable hydraulic design.
proposals.hypothesis: The scaling of air entrainment can be partitioned into distinct physical regimes, analogous to combustion regime diagrams, where specific dimensionless groups (Froude, Reynolds, Weber) dominate. Within each regime, a composite scaling law with explicit correction factors can be derived to provide accurate scale-up predictions.
proposals.method: The work will construct a three-dimensional regime diagram in Froude-Reynolds-Weber space. Systematic laboratory experiments on a stepped chute across a wide range of scales will provide air concentration and velocity data. Complementary high-fidelity numerical simulations using Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) methods with Large Eddy Simulation (LES) turbulence modelling will resolve interfacial dynamics. Data will be clustered to identify regime boundaries, and regime-specific scaling laws with correction factors will be calibrated using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a predictive, regime-based scaling framework that replaces ad-hoc corrections with a physically justified methodology. It will provide practitioners with a direct guide for applying laboratory results to prototype conditions, significantly improving the reliability of air-water flow predictions for hydraulic structure design.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of the combustion regime-diagram paradigm to hydraulic scaling problems represents a significant conceptual leap. It moves beyond seeking a single universal scaling correction to a piecewise, physics-driven framework that acknowledges multiple dominant mechanisms.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Similitude Framework for Air-Water Flow: Quantifying the Limits of Froude Scaling for Air Entrainment on Stepped Chutes
proposals.motivation: The design and safety assessment of high-velocity hydraulic structures, like stepped spillways, rely heavily on physical models scaled using Froude similitude. However, pre-2026 research by Chanson and others consistently shows scale effects in air-water flow properties (e.g., bubble size, interfacial area, energy dissipation), as viscous and surface tension forces, governed by Reynolds and Weber numbers, are not correctly scaled. This discrepancy introduces uncertainty in translating model data to prototype predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: A similitude framework that simultaneously relaxes strict Froude scaling to incorporate Reynolds and Weber number effects will provide a more accurate, predictive scaling law for key air entrainment parameters (air concentration, bubble frequency, specific interfacial area) in self-aerated flows on steep stepped chutes.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-scale experimental approach using geometrically similar stepped chute models at three distinct scales (e.g., 1:1, 1:10, 1:20) in a recirculating flume. Detailed air-water flow properties (void fraction, velocity, bubble count rate, chord length) will be measured using phase-detection intrusive probes (conductivity or optical fibre) across the entire flow depth for a range of discharges. Data analysis will focus on developing dimensionless correlations for key air-water parameters as functions of a combined scaling parameter (e.g., a product of powers of Froude, Reynolds, and Weber numbers), identified through systematic non-linear regression and dimensional analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical scaling methodology that quantifies the limits of Froude similitude for air-water flows. It will deliver improved correction functions for translating physical model data to prototype conditions, enhancing the predictive accuracy for energy dissipation and oxygen transfer in stepped spillway design. The results will directly inform hydraulic engineering practice and advance the fundamental understanding of scale effects in multiphase environmental flows.
proposals.novelty: While scale effects are acknowledged, this proposal systematically challenges the exclusive reliance on Froude scaling by developing and testing an explicit, combined scaling framework that integrates viscous and capillary effects, moving beyond qualitative discussion to quantitative, predictive correction laws.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework for Quantifying Scale Effects in Stepped Spillway Air-Water Flows: Controlled Intermediate-Scale Experiments Fused with Prototype Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of air-water flow properties (aeration, velocity, energy dissipation) on stepped spillways is critical for hydraulic design and safety. Current practice relies on physical models, but significant scale effects persist due to incomplete Froude scaling of Reynolds and Weber numbers, leading to uncertain extrapolation to prototypes. Existing validation is limited by the lack of controlled, systematic experiments at scales large enough to minimize these effects and by sparse, uncontrolled field data from operational dams.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic isolation of scaling parameters (Re, We, roughness) in a dedicated intermediate-scale facility, when fused with sparse prototype data within a Bayesian framework, will yield validated, uncertainty-quantified transfer functions for air-water flow properties, resolving the scale-effect dilemma for stepped chutes.
proposals.method: A new 1:5 to 1:10 scale stepped chute facility will be constructed with full instrumentation (phase-detection probes, LDA) and control of inflow conditions. Parametric experiments will isolate effects of Re, We, and step roughness. Concurrently, field campaigns at partner dam spillways will collect synchronized air-water flow data during scheduled releases. A hierarchical Bayesian model will be implemented (using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, e.g., Stan) to fuse these datasets, treating uncontrolled field conditions as hyperparameters and explicitly quantifying epistemic uncertainty from scaling biases versus prototype variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first rigorously validated and uncertainty-quantified correction functions for air-water flow scale effects on stepped spillways. It will deliver a robust methodological framework for fusing controlled laboratory and sparse field data, directly improving the reliability of physical modelling for hydraulic engineers. The new intermediate-scale dataset will serve as a benchmark for CFD validation.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a purpose-built, controlled intermediate-scale facility with a Bayesian data fusion framework specifically designed to disentangle and quantify the sources of scaling uncertainty, moving beyond qualitative comparisons to provide probabilistic predictive functions.

proposals.title: Field-Validated Similarity Scaling for Spillway Air-Water Flows: A Campaign-Based Approach Inspired by Atmospheric Boundary Layer Research
proposals.motivation: A critical, unresolved challenge in spillway hydraulics is the quantification of scale effects when extrapolating laboratory model results to prototype structures. Discrepancies in air entrainment, energy dissipation, and flow resistance between models and prototypes, due to Reynolds, Weber, and Froude number mismatches, lead to design uncertainties. Current prototype validation relies on sparse, single-point measurements, which are insufficient to characterize the complex, spatially heterogeneous nature of self-aerated turbulent flows.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, distributed field measurement campaigns on operational spillways will yield high-resolution datasets that enable the development of robust, semi-empirical similarity laws for air-water flows. These laws, analogous to Monin-Obukhov scaling in atmospheric science, will provide formally validated correction functions for laboratory-to-prototype scale effects.
proposals.method: The research will organize intensive field campaigns during controlled spillway gate openings, instrumenting the chute with distributed sensor arrays. These will include fiber-optic Distributed Temperature Sensing (DTS) for thermal tracing, Acoustic Doppler Velocity Profilers (ADV and ADCP) for 3D velocity fields, dual-tip conductivity probes for void fraction and bubble velocity, and drone-mounted LiDAR for free-surface topology. Data will be processed using ensemble averaging and quadrant analysis for turbulence statistics, then used to calibrate dimensionless scaling parameters (e.g., aeration efficiency, friction factor) as functions of key dimensionless numbers (Re, We, Fr) through multivariate regression and machine learning techniques (e.g., Random Forest regression).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first field-validated, generalized framework for correcting scale effects in spillway air-water flow models, directly improving the predictive accuracy of laboratory studies for prototype design. It will deliver a publicly accessible, high-resolution prototype dataset for validating numerical models (CFD) and advance the fundamental understanding of turbulence and aeration dynamics in high-velocity open channels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating the campaign-based validation paradigm from atmospheric science to hydraulic engineering and deploying a synchronized, distributed sensor network on a prototype spillway to move beyond point measurements. This approach explicitly seeks to develop spillway-specific similarity scaling, a concept not yet formally established in air-water flow hydraulics.

proposals.title: Bridging the Scale Gap: A Prototype-Scale, Data-Driven Framework for Predicting Scour Downstream of Stepped Spillways Using Air-Water Flow Signatures
proposals.motivation: Predicting sediment transport and scour downstream of high-velocity spillways remains a critical challenge in hydraulic engineering. Existing models, derived from Froude-scaled laboratory experiments, suffer from significant scale effects in air-water flow properties and turbulence, which directly influence bed shear stress. Consequently, prototype scour predictions are unreliable, posing risks to infrastructure safety and operational management.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-frequency, prototype-scale measurements of air-water flow parameters (void fraction, velocity spectra, dissipation) contain sufficient information to predict downstream sediment transport rates via machine learning, bypassing the need for scale-correction models that have never been validated against full-scale sediment response.
proposals.method: Dense sensor arrays (optical fibre probes, ADV, pressure transducers) will be deployed on an operational stepped spillway to capture synchronized, long-duration records of air-water flow properties and downstream bedload transport. This paired prototype dataset will train a multi-input neural network (e.g., a 1D convolutional neural network for temporal spectra and dense layers for bulk parameters) to regress sediment flux. Model inputs will include depth-averaged void fraction, turbulent kinetic energy, integral time scales, and energy dissipation rate, directly measured at prototype scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, data-driven model linking prototype air-water flow dynamics to sediment mobilization, eliminating reliance on unverified scale corrections. It will deliver an operational tool for spillway managers to forecast scour under variable discharges, fundamentally advancing the physical understanding of how self-aeration modulates sediment transport.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of prototype-scale, multiphase flow validation with machine learning for sediment prediction, a step never taken because such paired datasets do not exist. It explicitly tests the long-unverified assumption that laboratory-derived scaling laws for air-water flows are valid for sediment response.

proposals.title: Pressureless Dispersed-Phase Modeling of Air Entrainment in Stepped Spillways: An Astrophysical Two-Fluid Framework
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of air entrainment and energy dissipation on stepped chutes remains a central challenge in hydraulic engineering, as existing volume-of-fluid and mixture models struggle to resolve the full spectrum of entrained bubble scales and their coupling with turbulent water flow. Astrophysical simulations of dust-gas dynamics in protoplanetary disks face a structurally analogous problem—tracking a dispersed, nearly pressureless phase coupled to a carrier fluid through drag—and have developed robust Eulerian-Eulerian solvers with high-resolution shock-capturing schemes specifically designed for such systems. Transferring these mature numerical techniques to hydraulic air-water flows offers a principled path toward improved fidelity in self-aeration modeling without ad hoc empirical closures.
proposals.hypothesis: An Eulerian-Eulerian two-fluid model in which entrained air bubbles are treated as a pressureless dispersed phase coupled to the water continuum via interfacial drag—solved with astrophysical high-resolution schemes—will reproduce measured void fraction and velocity profiles on prototype stepped spillways more accurately than standard mixture or drift-flux models.
proposals.method: The governing equations from astrophysical dust-gas codes will be reformulated by replacing the dust phase with an air phase governed by bubble-scale drag correlations (Schiller-Naumann and Tomiyama models) and by introducing a k-epsilon turbulence closure for the water phase, including turbulent dispersion of bubbles. The hyperbolic system will be discretized using a modified HLLC Riemann solver augmented with fifth-order WENO reconstruction to handle the sharp void-fraction gradients at the inception point of self-aeration. The solver will be implemented within the open-source OpenFOAM framework, validated first against Chanson's laboratory stepped-chute datasets for void fraction, interfacial velocity, and bubble count rate, and then compared with prototype spillway observations to assess scale-effect performance. A systematic parameter study will quantify sensitivity to drag law selection, grid resolution at the pseudo-bottom, and turbulence model constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first hydraulic two-fluid solver that exploits the mathematical analogy between astrophysical dust dynamics and air entrainment, providing a physically grounded alternative to empirical aeration models. It will produce openly available benchmark comparisons against both laboratory and prototype stepped-chute data, establishing whether pressureless-phase treatments can capture scale effects that conventional models miss. The framework will be extensible to other aerated-flow contexts such as plunging jets, hydraulic jumps, and drop structures.
proposals.novelty: While Eulerian-Eulerian approaches exist in hydraulic CFD, none have adopted the pressureless-phase formulation or the specialized Riemann solvers developed for astrophysical dust-gas coupling. The cross-disciplinary transfer is non-obvious because hydraulic engineers typically assign a finite pressure to the air phase, whereas the astrophysical insight that the dispersed phase can be modeled as pressureless simplifies the eigenstructure and enables sharper resolution of entrainment fronts.

proposals.title: A Comparative Assessment of VOF and Eulerian Multiphase Models for Simulating Air Entrainment and Energy Dissipation on Stepped Spillways
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of air entrainment in high-velocity flows over hydraulic structures like stepped spillways is critical for estimating energy dissipation and assessing cavitation risks. Pre-2026, physical modelling remains the primary tool, but scaling effects and prototype extrapolation are challenging. Advanced Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) multiphase methods, well-established in chemical engineering, offer a promising but underexplored alternative for detailed, scale-independent analysis of these complex air-water interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method will accurately capture the sharp air-water interface and free-surface deformation in the non-aerated flow region, while an Eulerian multiphase (Euler-Euler) model will better simulate the dispersed bubble transport and void fraction distribution in the highly aerated flow region downstream of the inception point.
proposals.method: The study will employ the open-source CFD software OpenFOAM (v8 or earlier). Two distinct numerical setups will be configured for a validated stepped chute geometry: one using the interFoam solver (VOF with interface compression) and another using reactingTwoPhaseEulerFoam (Eulerian multifluid model with a k-ω SST turbulence closure). Simulations will be benchmarked against detailed prototype and laboratory data for air concentration, velocity profiles, and energy dissipation rates. A systematic comparison of computational cost, convergence behavior, and predictive accuracy for key hydraulic parameters will be conducted.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, practical guideline for selecting and applying appropriate CFD multiphase frameworks to model self-aerated flows on hydraulic structures. It will deliver new insights into the interplay between interface dynamics and dispersed bubble transport in high-velocity chute flows, directly informing the momentum and energy considerations central to Professor Chanson's recent research.
proposals.novelty: While VOF has seen some application in hydraulics, a direct, critical comparison with Eulerian multiphase models for stepped spillway flows is novel. The transfer and adaptation of the Eulerian multifluid approach, standard for chemical reactors, to explicitly model air bubble dispersion in hydraulic engineering represents a significant methodological crossover.

proposals.title: A Predictive Volume-of-Fluid Framework for Aerated Microfluidic Flows: Validation Against Micro-PIV and Application to Bubble Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The design of aerated microfluidic systems for lab-on-chip applications remains reliant on costly trial-and-error experimentation. While macro-scale air-water flow modelling is mature, the dominant capillary and viscous forces at the micro-scale present unique challenges for predictive simulation. A validated computational tool is urgently needed to enable the virtual prototyping of devices for biomedical diagnostics and chemical processing.
proposals.hypothesis: High-fidelity Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) and Eulerian multiphase methods, adapted from macro-scale hydraulic engineering, can accurately predict bubble formation, transport, and interfacial dynamics in microchannels when rigorously validated against microscale experimental data.
proposals.method: The research will adapt and implement an open-source VOF solver (e.g., OpenFOAM's interFoam) with interface compression and dynamic contact angle models. High-resolution micro-PIV and shadowgraphy experiments will provide validation data for bubble size, velocity, and shape in stepped and constricted micro-geometries. The numerical framework will be calibrated and tested for key dimensionless numbers (We, Ca, Re) relevant to microfluidic two-phase flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source simulation framework specifically validated for aerated microfluidic flows, reducing design iteration time. It will provide new insights into the scaling of air entrainment and energy dissipation from macro-scale chutes to micro-scale channels, bridging Hubert Chanson's expertise with microfluidic engineering.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation and rigorous validation of macro-scale, self-aeration CFD methodologies for the micro-scale, where surface tension dominates, is a novel synthesis. This creates a new predictive design tool for a domain traditionally dominated by experimental microfluidics.

proposals.title: Interfacial Dynamics and Energy Dissipation in Micro-Scale Aerated Flows: A Lab-on-Chip Analogue to Stepped Chute Hydraulics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the fundamental understanding of air-water flow dynamics, including self-aeration and energy dissipation, is derived from large-scale hydraulic structures like stepped spillways, where gravity and turbulence dominate. The scaling of these processes to microfluidic regimes, where surface tension and viscous forces prevail, remains unexplored. Investigating this scale transition is crucial for developing novel lab-on-chip applications in chemical processing, biomedical diagnostics, and micro-scale cooling, where precise control of two-phase flow energy transfer is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The fundamental mechanisms of air entrainment, bubble transport, and interfacial energy dissipation observed in macro-scale stepped chute flows can be directly scaled to microfluidic channels, but with the dominant force balance shifting from gravity and inertia to capillary and viscous forces, leading to distinct, predictable flow regimes.
proposals.method: The research will employ precision microfluidic fabrication (soft lithography in PDMS) to create stepped micro-channels with characteristic dimensions from 50 to 500 µm. High-speed micro-Particle Image Velocimetry (µPIV) and optical shadowgraphy will measure velocity fields and bubble characteristics. The experimental protocol will systematically vary flow rate, step geometry, and fluid properties (surface tension, viscosity). Data analysis will focus on quantifying air concentration profiles, bubble size distributions, and energy dissipation rates, comparing them directly to established scaling laws from macro-scale hydraulic models (e.g., Chanson's formulations for self-aerated flows) to identify modified dimensionless parameters (e.g., capillary number vs. Froude number).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct experimental link between macro-scale hydraulic air-water flow theory and microfluidic two-phase flow, producing new scaling relationships for aerated flows in capillary-dominated systems. It will provide a validated framework for designing microfluidic devices that exploit controlled aeration for enhanced mixing, reaction, or thermal management, thereby bridging the fields of environmental hydraulics and micro-engineering.
proposals.novelty: While Hubert Chanson's work has extensively characterized aeration in prototype and large-scale physical models, applying these core momentum and energy principles to the micro-scale, where his established gravity-dominated models break down, is a novel and untested frontier. The proposal uniquely posits that the phenomenological understanding from stepped chutes provides the correct conceptual template for analyzing micro-scale aerated flows, despite the shift in governing forces.

proposals.title: Inverse Tomography of Turbulence: Reconstructing Velocity Fields from Air Concentration Data in Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: In hydraulic engineering, the self-aeration process in high-velocity flows like those over stepped chutes is a critical indicator of turbulence intensity and energy dissipation. Current methods rely on point measurements of air concentration and velocity, which provide limited spatial integration and struggle to characterize the underlying three-dimensional turbulent structures responsible for air entrainment and transport. A method to infer the full turbulent flow field from scalar concentration data would revolutionize the analysis of prototype and model-scale chutes, linking aeration directly to turbulence statistics.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially distributed air concentration field in a self-aerated flow contains sufficient integrated information about the advective and diffusive turbulent transport processes to allow for the statistical reconstruction of key turbulence properties, including the turbulent kinetic energy spectrum and dissipation rate, via an inverse tomographic framework.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical modelling and computational approach. High-resolution air concentration data will be collected in a laboratory stepped chute using an array of phase-detection probes, providing multiple spatial viewpoints. This data will serve as the input for an inverse algorithm formulated around the advection-diffusion equation for a passive scalar. The algorithm will leverage established tomographic reconstruction techniques (e.g., Algebraic Reconstruction Technique - ART) adapted to solve for the turbulent diffusivity and velocity fluctuation fields that minimize the difference between simulated and measured concentration distributions. Validation will be performed against simultaneous Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) measurements in non-aerated analogous flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, non-intrusive diagnostic tool for inferring turbulence characteristics from readily measurable air concentration data in hydraulic models and prototypes. It will advance the fundamental understanding of the relationship between scalar transport and turbulence in free-surface flows, offering a new methodology to validate numerical models of aerated flows and improve energy dissipation predictions on stepped spillways.
proposals.novelty: While tomography is established in other fields, its application to infer turbulence statistics from a passive scalar (air) in free-surface hydraulic flows is unprecedented. It moves beyond correlation-based analyses to a formal inverse problem that statistically reconstructs the causative velocity field from its scalar effect.
