proposals.title: Inverse Modelling of Turbulence from Air Concentration Profiles: A Physics-Informed Data Assimilation Framework for Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: Accurate turbulence modelling in self-aerated flows over hydraulic structures like stepped chutes remains a critical challenge for predictive CFD. While air concentration profiles are commonly measured in physical models, directly linking these data to the underlying 3D turbulent velocity and energy fields is difficult. This gap limits the validation and improvement of RANS closure models (e.g., k-ε) for prototype-scale predictions, where detailed velocity measurements are often infeasible.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep inverse model, trained on high-fidelity multiphase CFD data, can infer the spatial fields of turbulent kinetic energy (k) and its dissipation rate (ε) directly from measurable air concentration profiles. This inferred turbulence state can then be assimilated into a RANS solver to correct its closure model and improve predictive accuracy for air-water flow properties.
proposals.method: High-resolution Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) simulations of stepped chute flows will generate a comprehensive synthetic dataset linking 3D velocity, turbulence (k, ε), and air concentration (C) fields. A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture will be trained as an inverse model to map 2D air concentration profiles (C(y,z)) to the corresponding 3D k and ε fields, with the RANS equations embedded as soft constraints. This trained PINN will be integrated into an OpenFOAM-based data assimilation loop, where sparse experimental C measurements will be used to infer and impose corrected k/ε fields, guiding the solver's turbulence model towards a more physically consistent solution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-intrusive framework for assimilating readily available experimental data (air concentration) directly into CFD turbulence closure. It will provide a new methodology for rigorously calibrating and improving RANS models for aerated hydraulic flows, moving beyond simple parameter tuning. The resulting tool will enhance the predictive reliability of CFD for the design and analysis of prototype stepped spillways and chutes.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely uses high-fidelity multiphase CFD not for direct prediction, but as a rich data generator to train an inverse model that bridges the gap between measurable air concentration and unmeasurable prototype turbulence fields. Fusing this inverse model with a data assimilation loop for RANS correction is a novel application of physics-informed machine learning in hydraulic engineering.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Turbulent Velocity Fields from Air Concentration Profiles in Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows
proposals.motivation: In stepped spillway design, self-aeration is critical for energy dissipation and cavitation protection, but direct measurement of the turbulent velocity field in high-velocity aerated flows is notoriously difficult and intrusive. Air concentration profiles are more readily measured in both physical models and prototypes. This research addresses the inverse problem: using these accessible aeration data to infer the hidden turbulence structure, providing a novel diagnostic tool for hydraulic engineers.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of air concentration in self-aerated flows on steep stepped chutes is a direct, invertible function of the underlying turbulence characteristics, specifically the turbulent kinetic energy and dissipation rate fields, allowing for their quantitative reconstruction.
proposals.method: The approach will combine detailed physical modelling with inverse analysis. High-resolution air concentration and velocity data will be collected in a laboratory stepped chute using phase-detection probes and PIV/LDV. A forward model, based on the advection-diffusion equation for air bubbles with a turbulent diffusivity linked to local turbulence properties, will be developed. The inverse problem will be solved using an iterative gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt) to adjust the turbulent velocity field parameters in the model until the predicted air concentration profile matches the measured one.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, non-intrusive methodology for inferring key turbulence parameters from aeration data, enhancing the analysis of prototype spillway performance from limited field measurements. It will deepen the fundamental understanding of the momentum and energy transfer mechanisms between the turbulent carrier flow and the dispersed air phase, a core theme in Chanson's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While existing research correlates aeration and turbulence, the explicit formulation and solution of this inverse problem—treating air concentration as the input to reconstruct the velocity field—is a novel inversion of the standard analytical perspective. It moves beyond correlation to establish a causative, quantitative inference framework.

proposals.title: Active De-aeration of Pressurized Hydraulic Conduits via Controlled Pressure Transients: An Analogy from Medical Ultrasound Cavitation Control
proposals.motivation: In pressurized hydraulic systems, such as water supply pipelines and hydropower penstocks, the presence of entrained air can lead to efficiency losses, measurement errors, and structural damage from water hammer. Traditional passive de-aeration systems are often inefficient for finely dispersed microbubbles. A novel, active control method is needed to enhance air removal, improving system reliability and energy efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: That controlled, low-energy pressure transients or ultrasonic excitations, designed using dynamic similarity principles from cavitation bubble dynamics, can actively nucleate and coalesce microscopic air pockets in water, driving them to designated vents for efficient removal.
proposals.method: The research will employ physical modelling in a closed, pressurized conduit test rig. Controlled pressure waveforms, designed using a modified Rayleigh-Plesset framework to target specific bubble nuclei sizes, will be generated via a fast-acting valve or piezoelectric actuator. High-speed videography and phase-detection intrusive probes will quantify air-water flow properties (void fraction, bubble size distribution, velocity) upstream and downstream of the excitation zone. The methodology will systematically vary excitation frequency, amplitude, and flow conditions (Reynolds number) to establish scaling laws for effective de-aeration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physics-based framework for active de-aeration, moving beyond passive systems. It will deliver quantitative design guidelines for applying controlled excitations in conduits, contributing directly to the hydraulic engineering literature on air-water flow management and energy dissipation in closed systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of medical High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU) cavitation control principles—a well-established field—to the inverse problem of air removal in hydraulic engineering, a cross-disciplinary application not previously explored.

proposals.title: Field Validation of Lateral Hyporheic Flow Penetration in Pool-Riffle Streams: A Tracer-Based Assessment of the 20% Travel Distance Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Hyporheic exchange is critical for nutrient cycling, contaminant attenuation, and stream ecosystem health. While conceptual and numerical models often predict lateral hyporheic flow penetration distances relative to stream width (e.g., 20%), these predictions lack robust field validation, particularly in natural pool-riffle sequences where complex flow dynamics drive exchange. Pre-2026, there is a significant gap between theoretical predictions derived from idealized models and empirical data from real, morphologically complex streams.
proposals.hypothesis: In natural pool-riffle streams, the lateral penetration distance of hyporheic flow, quantified by tracer breakthrough, will consistently approximate 20% of the stream width, validating a key scaling relationship for predicting hyporheic zone extent.
proposals.method: The study will conduct in-situ tracer experiments in selected pool-riffle streams. A conservative tracer (e.g., NaCl or fluorescent dye) will be injected into the surface water at the head of a riffle. An array of mini-piezometers and multilevel samplers will be installed laterally from the bank into the streambed to monitor tracer concentration in the subsurface. Breakthrough curves will be analyzed using temporal moment analysis and a 1D advection-dispersion model to quantify lateral travel distances and exchange rates, directly comparing results to the predicted 20% of local stream width.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct field validation of a widely used scaling rule for lateral hyporheic exchange, moving beyond theoretical and flume-based studies. It will yield a dataset linking stream morphology (pool-riffle geometry) to measurable hyporheic extent, improving the parameterization of reach-scale nutrient processing models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct field-testing of a specific, predicted scaling ratio (20%) using detailed subsurface tracer measurements in natural pool-riffle systems, a validation step conspicuously absent in pre-2026 literature which often relies on indirect methods or idealized settings.

proposals.title: Beyond the Steady-State Paradigm: Time-Resolved Analysis of Air–Water Flow Dynamics on Stepped Chutes
proposals.motivation: The analysis of self-aerated flows in hydraulic engineering, particularly on steep stepped chutes, has long relied on time-averaged measurements of air concentration and velocity. While this steady-state assumption is practical, it obscures the inherent, rapid fluctuations in flow structure and aeration that occur at the scale of individual steps and macro-roughness elements. A pre-2026 understanding acknowledges that these unsteady processes are critical for accurate modeling of energy dissipation, flow resistance, and extreme pressure loads, yet they remain poorly quantified due to a lack of suitable high-frequency measurement techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-averaged properties of aerated flows on stepped chutes are insufficient descriptors of the flow physics; significant, coherent fluctuations in air–water phase distribution and velocity exist at characteristic frequencies linked to step geometry and flow rate, fundamentally influencing local and reach-scale energy dissipation.
proposals.method: The research will employ high-speed, dual-tip phase-detection probes and ultra-high-speed videography (≥ 2000 fps) synchronized with a traversing system to capture time-resolved air–water interface velocities and concentrations at multiple points over a stepped chute in a large-scale laboratory flume. Data analysis will move beyond simple time-averaging to implement spectral analysis (FFT) and wavelet transforms to identify dominant fluctuation frequencies. Conditional sampling and quadrant analysis techniques, adapted from turbulent boundary layer research, will be used to decompose the signal into coherent structures (e.g., air packet advection, water droplet ejection) and quantify their contribution to the total momentum and energy flux.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, time-resolved dataset on the kinematics of unsteady aerated flows on stepped chutes. It will yield new metrics for characterizing flow unsteadiness and establish quantitative links between instantaneous flow structures and time-averaged energy dissipation models. The findings will challenge and refine the application of steady-flow principles in spillway design and safety assessment.
proposals.novelty: While Professor Chanson's prior work has expertly characterized mean flow properties, this proposal directly targets the *temporal structure* of the fluctuations themselves, applying signal processing techniques common in turbulence research but novel in the context of aerated stepped chute hydraulics to deconstruct the steady-state assumption.

proposals.title: Quantifying Scale Effects in Air-Water Flow Properties: A Direct Comparison of Phase-Detection Probe Data from Prototype Stepped Spillways and Distorted Physical Models
proposals.motivation: The design of high-velocity hydraulic structures like stepped spillways relies on physical modelling to predict air entrainment, which is crucial for energy dissipation and cavitation protection. However, significant scale effects are suspected, as Froude-scaled models cannot simultaneously satisfy Reynolds and Weber number similarity, potentially misrepresenting bubble breakup and interfacial processes. This discrepancy can lead to non-conservative designs if model aeration data are extrapolated directly to prototype scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Phase-detection probe measurements in Froude-scaled laboratory models systematically overestimate the local air concentration and underestimate the bubble count rate and interfacial velocity in the turbulent air-water shear layer compared to prototype flows, due to unresolved viscous and surface tension scale effects.
proposals.method: The research will conduct synchronized field and laboratory experiments on an identical stepped chute geometry. Prototype data will be collected using dual-tip fiber-optic phase-detection probes at a large dam spillway. Concurrently, a precisely Froude-scaled (e.g., 1:10 to 1:20) physical model will be instrumented with geometrically scaled probes. The analysis will focus on comparing key derived properties: void fraction, bubble count rate, interfacial velocity, and chord length distributions across identical dimensionless step positions. Dimensional analysis and comparative statistics will be used to develop empirical correction functions for key aeration parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, direct dataset quantifying scale distortions in air-water flow measurements for stepped chutes. It will yield practical correction guidelines for hydraulic engineers to adjust model-derived aeration data for prototype predictions. The findings will advance fundamental understanding of two-phase flow scaling, informing future modelling standards for aerated flows.
proposals.novelty: While scale effects are acknowledged, this study proposes a rare, direct side-by-side quantification using identical instrumentation and analysis on prototype and model, moving beyond indirect comparisons. It specifically targets the performance of phase-detection probes—the standard laboratory tool—in a real-world scaling context, which has not been systematically done.

proposals.title: From Bubbles to Droplets: Adapting Phase-Detection Probe Signal Processing for Water Droplet Characterization in Gas-Dominated Multiphase Flows
proposals.motivation: Phase-detection intrusive probes have been extensively developed for characterizing air bubble entrainment in high-velocity free-surface flows, yielding robust frameworks for void fraction, bubble frequency, and chord length distributions. However, the mathematically dual problem—detecting discrete water droplets carried within a continuous gas phase, as encountered in spray zones above stepped spillways, cooling towers, and industrial atomization—lacks an equivalent rigorous signal-processing methodology. Bridging this gap would unify two-phase flow instrumentation across the full void fraction spectrum from bubbly to spray regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: The signal-processing framework established for air-bubble detection in water flows (threshold-based phase discrimination, chord length analysis, and clustering algorithms) can be systematically inverted and recalibrated to yield accurate droplet size distributions, liquid fraction profiles, and droplet frequency measurements in gas-dominated flows, provided that probe tip geometry and threshold criteria are adapted to account for the reversed wetting behavior and different interfacial curvature scales.
proposals.method: The study will first derive the theoretical duality between bubble-in-liquid and droplet-in-gas probe signals by analyzing the governing equations for phase indicator functions under reversed continuous-phase conditions. A dual-tip conductivity probe will be operated in controlled spray jets with known droplet size distributions (measured independently via laser diffraction) across liquid fractions from 0.01 to 0.30. Signal processing algorithms—single-threshold, adaptive-threshold, and wavelet-based decomposition—will be benchmarked against the reference measurements. Chord length-to-diameter conversion factors will be recalculated using Monte Carlo simulations of randomly distributed spherical droplets intersecting cylindrical probe tips, accounting for droplet deformation at Weber numbers relevant to spillway spray zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source signal-processing toolbox that extends the well-established air-water probe methodology to droplet-in-gas measurements, enabling consistent instrumentation across the full range of multiphase flow regimes encountered in hydraulic engineering. It will provide the first systematic comparison of threshold selection criteria for the dual problem and establish correction factors for chord-to-diameter conversion specific to spray-regime droplet populations.
proposals.novelty: While probe-based techniques for bubbly flows are mature, no prior study has formally exploited the mathematical duality of the phase-detection signal to construct and validate a mirror framework for the spray regime. The explicit derivation of dual threshold criteria and geometry-specific chord length corrections for droplets, rather than bubbles, represents a non-obvious inversion that addresses an instrumentation blind spot in hydraulic and industrial multiphase flow research.

proposals.title: Rheological Characterization of Dense Bubble-Laden Flows on Stepped Chutes: Implications for Energy Dissipation Models
proposals.motivation: Current hydraulic models for highly aerated flows, such as those over stepped spillways, treat the air-water mixture as a Newtonian fluid. However, at high air concentrations, the dense bubble swarm may exhibit complex interactions, potentially leading to non-Newtonian behavior (e.g., shear-thinning, viscoelasticity). This gap challenges the accuracy of momentum and energy dissipation predictions, which are critical for spillway design and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dense, highly aerated flows (air concentration > 30%) exhibit measurable non-Newtonian rheological properties, and that incorporating these properties into a two-phase flow model will significantly improve the prediction of energy dissipation rates on steep, stepped chutes.
proposals.method: The research will combine physical modelling and rheometric analysis. A recirculating flume with a stepped chute will generate flows with controlled air concentrations. A custom-designed pressure-coupled vane rheometer, integrated into the chute bed, will measure in-situ shear stress versus shear rate. Concurrently, detailed air-water flow properties (velocity, concentration, bubble size) will be measured using phase-detection intrusive probes and high-speed imaging. The data will be used to calibrate a two-fluid model where the bubbly phase is described by a Herschel-Bulkley or power-law constitutive relation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence and a constitutive model for the non-Newtonian rheology of highly aerated hydraulic flows. It will advance fundamental two-phase flow theory and deliver a refined modelling framework for engineers to more accurately predict energy dissipation and flow dynamics on aerated spillways.
proposals.novelty: While non-Newtonian behavior is studied in sediment-laden or debris flows, its systematic investigation in dense bubble-laden water flows is novel. The direct in-situ rheometry of a highly aerated hydraulic flow on a stepped chute represents a significant methodological advancement beyond standard air-water property measurements.

proposals.title: A novel application of nuclear-grade two-phase CFD models for high-resolution simulation of air-water shear and energy dissipation in hydraulic jumps
proposals.motivation: The accurate prediction of air-water shear and turbulence in hydraulic jumps is critical for assessing energy dissipation and structural loads in spillways and stilling basins. Existing hydraulic models often rely on simplified assumptions for bubble dynamics and interfacial transfer. Concurrently, the nuclear thermal-hydraulics community has developed and rigorously validated advanced two-phase CFD models for critical safety analyses, but these have not been systematically applied to free-surface hydraulic engineering problems.
proposals.hypothesis: High-fidelity, validated multiphase CFD models developed for nuclear reactor thermal-hydraulics—specifically the Eulerian-Eulerian Two-Fluid Model with interfacial closure laws—can be successfully transferred to simulate the complex air-water shear and phase distribution in hydraulic jumps with greater physical fidelity than standard hydraulic CFD approaches.
proposals.method: The research will implement and adapt the NEPTUNE_CFD or OpenFOAM-based Two-Fluid Model, utilizing pre-2025 validated closure laws for drag, lift, and turbulent dispersion forces from nuclear bubbly flow studies. The Interfacial Area Transport (IAT) equation will be employed to dynamically model bubble size distribution. Simulations will be conducted for well-documented hydraulic jump experiments (e.g., from Chanson's own laboratory data) to calibrate and validate the model. Key validation metrics will include void fraction profiles, velocity fields, and turbulent kinetic energy dissipation rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physically rigorous computational tool for hydraulic engineers to predict energy dissipation and aeration in hydraulic structures. It will establish a direct methodological bridge between nuclear thermal-hydraulics and free-surface hydraulics, enabling cross-disciplinary validation. The results will offer deeper insight into the momentum and energy transfer mechanisms within the highly turbulent air-water shear layer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate cross-pollination of a mature, high-stakes modelling framework from nuclear safety into civil hydraulic engineering, applying its sophisticated treatment of two-phase interfacial physics to the distinct flow regime of a hydraulic jump, which has not been done systematically before 2026.

proposals.title: Air-Water Shear Flow Dynamics Under Extreme Flood Discharges: Scaling Laws and Energy Dissipation Beyond Current Design Envelopes on Stepped Spillways
proposals.motivation: Modern dam spillways are increasingly subjected to flood discharges that exceed their original design parameters due to revised probable maximum flood estimates driven by climate variability. Existing air-water flow characterization on stepped chutes, including Chanson's extensive physical modeling work, has been validated only up to moderate prototype unit discharges (typically below 25-30 m²/s), leaving a critical knowledge gap for extreme events where cavitation risk, spray generation, and energy dissipation efficiency may deviate substantially from established scaling relationships. Understanding these extreme regimes is essential for infrastructure safety and for updating design guidelines worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: At extreme prototype unit discharges exceeding 30 m²/s on stepped spillways, the self-aeration onset location, air-water interfacial velocity profiles, and turbulent shear stress distributions deviate systematically from predictions of existing Froude-scaled physical models due to non-negligible Weber and Reynolds number scale effects, resulting in reduced energy dissipation efficiency compared to current design assumptions.
proposals.method: A dual-scale experimental campaign will be conducted using two geometrically similar stepped chute facilities at scales 1:10 and 1:25 to isolate Reynolds and Weber number scale effects at equivalent prototype discharges of 30-60 m²/s. Dual-tip phase-detection conductivity probes and fiber-optical sensors will capture void fraction, bubble frequency, interfacial velocity, and turbulence intensity profiles across the full flow depth at multiple stations downstream of inception. Dimensional analysis following Chanson's momentum-energy framework will be applied to derive modified scaling correction factors that account for surface tension and viscous effects at extreme flow conditions. Complementary computational fluid dynamics simulations using a Volume-of-Fluid method with adaptive mesh refinement will extend the parameter space beyond experimental limits and validate the proposed correction relationships against prototype spillway observations from existing dam monitoring databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic dataset of air-water flow properties at unit discharges representative of extreme flood scenarios on stepped spillways, filling a critical gap in the hydraulic engineering literature. The derived scale-effect correction factors will enable practitioners to reliably extrapolate laboratory measurements to extreme prototype conditions, directly informing updated spillway design standards and dam safety risk assessments.
proposals.novelty: While prior scale-effect studies have focused on moderate discharges within current design envelopes, no systematic investigation has characterized air-water shear flow behavior at discharges exceeding design thresholds where multiple dimensionless number similitude breaks down simultaneously, nor has a unified correction framework been proposed that couples momentum-energy analysis with dual-scale experimental validation at these extreme conditions.

proposals.title: Dual-Phase Analogy: Water Jet Breakup in Air as a Mirror Problem to Air Entrainment in Hydraulic Flows
proposals.motivation: Air entrainment in high-velocity free-surface flows over stepped chutes and spillways remains incompletely understood despite decades of research by Chanson and others, partly because the turbulent fragmentation interface is difficult to characterize from the water side alone. The dual problem—a water jet fragmenting in air—offers a complementary vantage point where the dispersed phase (water droplets) and the continuous phase (air) are reversed, enabling measurement techniques (high-speed imaging, laser diffraction) that are often impractical in bubbly flows. Establishing a rigorous analogy between these two configurations could unlock transferable scaling laws and improve energy dissipation predictions on prototype hydraulic structures.
proposals.hypothesis: The size distributions and entrainment rates observed during water jet breakup in air follow statistically analogous fragmentation scaling laws to those governing air bubble entrainment in self-aerated free-surface flows, with a mappable correspondence in Weber number, turbulent Reynolds number, and void fraction (or liquid fraction) profiles.
proposals.method: Laboratory experiments will be conducted using a vertical water jet (diameters 5–25 mm) discharging into quiescent air at velocities spanning Weber numbers from 100 to 10,000, matching the range encountered in stepped chute self-aeration. High-speed imaging (5,000+ fps) coupled with shadowgraphy will capture droplet size distributions, breakup lengths, and spray cone geometry. Phase-detection conductivity probes, identical to those used in Chanson's air-water flow studies, will be adapted in an inverted configuration to measure liquid fraction profiles in the spray region. Results will be compared directly against existing air-water flow datasets from stepped chutes using dimensionless void fraction and bubble/droplet chord length distributions. A theoretical framework based on turbulent fragmentation theory (Hinze-Kolmogorov cascade) will be developed to unify both problems through a symmetry transformation of the governing two-phase transport equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic experimental validation of the dual-phase analogy between water jet breakup and air entrainment in hydraulic engineering flows. It will deliver transferable scaling relationships that allow air-water flow properties on prototype spillways to be inferred from the more easily measured jet breakup problem, and provide a unified fragmentation framework applicable to both configurations.
proposals.novelty: While jet breakup and air entrainment have each been studied extensively in isolation, no prior work has systematically exploited the mathematical duality between these two problems using matched dimensionless parameters and identical instrumentation in both configurations, bridging atomization science and hydraulic engineering.

proposals.title: Inverting the Dissipation Paradigm: A Design Framework for Stepped Chutes to Maximize Turbulent Aeration and Gas Transfer
proposals.motivation: Stepped spillways are primarily studied for their energy dissipation capacity, but their intense turbulence also drives air entrainment and gas transfer, critical for water quality (e.g., oxygen, CO2) in downstream ecosystems and reservoirs. Pre-2026 research by Chanson and others has characterized aeration as a byproduct of dissipation, yet no systematic design methodology exists to intentionally maximize aeration. This gap limits the potential application of stepped channels as engineered systems for re-aeration and volatile contaminant stripping.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically varying step geometry, slope, and flow conditions, a stepped chute can be optimized not for minimum residual energy, but for maximum turbulent kinetic energy flux and air-water interfacial area, thereby maximizing gas transfer efficiency.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical modelling and analytical approach. Detailed laboratory experiments on a large-scale stepped flume will measure air-water flow properties (void fraction, bubble count rate, interfacial velocity) using phase-detection probes, correlating them with gas transfer rates measured via oxygen re-aeration tests. The momentum and energy framework from Chanson's recent work will be inverted to develop a new predictive model, where key parameters (step height, length, slope) are optimized using a multi-objective algorithm to maximize aeration performance metrics derived from the experimental data, rather than minimize residual head.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated design framework for 'aeration-optimized' stepped channels, shifting their role from purely dissipative structures to multi-functional water quality enhancers. It will deliver predictive equations linking step geometry to gas transfer coefficients, offering practical tools for hydraulic engineers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fundamentally inverting the classic stepped chute design problem from energy dissipation to energy addition (via gas transfer), and in developing a formal optimization methodology for aeration, which has only been treated descriptively in prior literature.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Aerospace-Derived Turbulence Closure Models for Self-Aerated Stepped Chute Flows: A Systematic CFD-to-Prototype Validation
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of air-water flow properties on stepped spillways remains limited by the turbulence models available in hydraulic CFD practice, which overwhelmingly rely on standard k-epsilon or k-omega SST closures calibrated for single-phase wall-bounded flows. Aerospace engineering has developed advanced Reynolds stress models (RSM), hybrid RANS-LES approaches such as Detached Eddy Simulation (DES), and explicit algebraic stress models (EASM) that capture anisotropic turbulence, streamline curvature, and separated shear layers with far greater fidelity. Transferring and validating these closures against prototype-scale stepped chute data would directly address the persistent gap between physical model observations and numerical predictions highlighted by Chanson's recent work on self-aeration and energy dissipation.
proposals.hypothesis: Aerospace-derived turbulence closures—specifically the Launder-Reece-Rodi RSM and Delayed DES—will reproduce prototype air concentration profiles, velocity distributions, and energy dissipation rates on steep stepped chutes with significantly lower error than standard two-equation models, because they better resolve the turbulent shear and flow separation at step edges that drive air entrainment.
proposals.method: We will implement four turbulence closures in OpenFOAM's interFoam VOF solver: (1) standard k-omega SST as baseline, (2) Launder-Reece-Rodi full RSM, (3) an explicit algebraic Reynolds stress variant (EASM), and (4) Delayed Detached Eddy Simulation (DDES). Each model will simulate a 1:1 prototype-scale stepped chute geometry matching Chanson's published field measurements, with identical mesh resolution (y+ < 1 near walls, at least 10 cells per step cavity) and boundary conditions. We will quantify performance using mean absolute error of void fraction profiles at multiple stations, velocity distributions, residual energy at the chute toe, and computational cost. A systematic sensitivity analysis on mesh density, time-step, and inlet turbulence intensity will accompany each closure to isolate modelling error from numerical error.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first systematic, prototype-validated comparison of aerospace turbulence closures applied to self-aerated stepped spillway flows, providing hydraulic engineers with quantitative guidance on which models justify their computational overhead. The resulting best-practice recommendations and openly released OpenFOAM case files will lower the barrier for adopting advanced CFD in spillway design and safety assessment.
proposals.novelty: While individual advanced closures have occasionally appeared in hydraulic CFD studies, no work has benchmarked a coherent family of aerospace-heritage models—RSM, EASM, and DDES—against full-scale stepped chute prototype data with controlled numerical error, bridging the aerospace-hydraulics transfer gap that Chanson's experimental comparisons have exposed.

proposals.title: A Unified Momentum-Energy Framework for Planetary-Scale Open Channel Flows: From Earth's Mega-Rivers to Martian Outflow Channels
proposals.motivation: Classical open channel hydraulics, developed for engineered and natural channels on Earth, faces fundamental scaling challenges when applied to continental-scale river systems, mega-tidal estuaries, and extraterrestrial features like Martian outflow channels. These systems operate at scales where assumptions of uniform roughness, negligible air entrainment effects, and standard resistance laws may break down, limiting predictive capability for paleo-hydrology and landscape evolution. A physics-based scaling framework is urgently needed to interpret these planetary-scale geomorphic records.
proposals.hypothesis: The momentum and energy principles governing self-aerated, highly-turbulent flows on steep stepped chutes can be reformulated into a scale-invariant framework, capable of describing the bulk hydraulic behaviour of planetary-scale channels where extreme Reynolds numbers, variable fluid properties (e.g., Martian atmosphere), and complex boundary interactions dominate.
proposals.method: The research will extend the momentum and energy analysis from stepped chute flows (Chanson 2020, 2021) by developing dimensionless governing equations incorporating scale-dependent friction factors and air-water mixture properties. The method will apply a staged physical modelling approach: 1) Laboratory data from high-velocity flows on rough and stepped invert will establish baseline scaling laws for friction and aeration. 2) These laws will be incorporated into a 1D numerical solver (e.g., based on the Saint-Venant equations but with modified resistance and energy dissipation terms) and validated against prototype data from Earth's largest rivers (e.g., Amazon, Brahmaputra) and estuaries (e.g., Bay of Fundy). 3) The validated model will be applied to Martian channel morphologies using pre-2026 topographic data (e.g., MOLA, HRSC) and adjusted for Martian gravity and atmospheric density to reconstruct paleo-discharges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel hydraulic scaling tool that bridges the gap between laboratory-scale turbulence physics and planetary geomorphology. It will yield improved paleo-discharge estimates for Martian outflow channels, offering new constraints on the planet's hydrological history. Furthermore, it will advance the fundamental understanding of energy dissipation and flow resistance in ultra-large-scale natural conduits on Earth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly adapting the momentum-energy framework from highly-aerated, turbulent stepped chute flows—a domain of detailed laboratory study—to the inverse problem of interpreting planetary-scale channel morphology, a non-obvious cross-scale application. This moves beyond simple Manning's equation scaling by incorporating scale-dependent aeration and turbulence effects.

proposals.title: High-Resolution PIV Mapping of Recirculation Zones and Turbulent Structures on Stepped Spillway Chutes
proposals.motivation: Stepped spillways are widely used for energy dissipation in dam engineering, yet the internal flow structure within step cavities remains poorly characterized due to limitations of intrusive instrumentation such as conductivity probes. Understanding the velocity fields and recirculation dynamics within these cavities is critical for optimizing step geometry and predicting energy dissipation performance, especially as discrepancies persist between physical model results and prototype observations documented by Chanson and colleagues.
proposals.hypothesis: Time-resolved particle image velocimetry (PIV) applied to stepped chute flows will reveal that recirculation zone geometry and turbulent kinetic energy distribution within step cavities deviate significantly from assumptions in existing semi-empirical models, and that these deviations correlate with the onset of self-aeration and the rate of energy dissipation along the chute.
proposals.method: A large-scale laboratory stepped chute with transparent sidewalls will be constructed at Reynolds numbers exceeding 10^5 to minimize scale effects identified in prior physical modelling studies. A planar time-resolved PIV system (dual-pulsed Nd:YAG laser with high-speed CMOS cameras at 1 kHz) will capture instantaneous two-component velocity fields in the sagittal plane of multiple step cavities across skimming, transition, and nappe flow regimes. Fluorescent seeding particles (Rhodamine B-doped polyamide, 20-50 μm) combined with optical longpass filters will be used to discriminate particle signal from air-bubble reflections in the aerated flow region. Phase-averaging conditioned on air-water interface position, detected via synchronized conductivity probes, will separate the clear-water and aerated zones. Proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) will extract dominant coherent structures, and Reynolds stress tensor fields will be compared against RANS and LES computational predictions available in the literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, non-intrusive, spatially resolved velocity field measurements inside stepped chute recirculation cavities across multiple flow regimes. The dataset will serve as a high-fidelity benchmark for validating CFD models of stepped spillways and will quantitatively link cavity vortex dynamics to bulk energy dissipation rates, bridging the gap between Chanson's momentum-energy framework and local turbulence mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While PIV has been applied to smooth spillways and isolated steps, no study has systematically resolved the full recirculation velocity field across flow regimes on stepped chutes at high Reynolds numbers with simultaneous air-water discrimination. The combination of phase-averaged PIV with POD analysis in self-aerating stepped flows is unprecedented and addresses the long-standing challenge of optical access in bubbly hydraulic flows.

proposals.title: Scale Effects on Air-Water Flow Dynamics and Energy Dissipation for Mega-Scale RCC Stepped Spillways: A Physical Modelling and Momentum-Energy Framework
proposals.motivation: The global trend towards constructing Roller-Compacted Concrete (RCC) dams of unprecedented height (>200m) creates a critical design gap. Existing stepped spillway guidelines, largely based on physical models at conventional scales, may not accurately predict the self-aeration, dynamic pressure loads, and energy dissipation on mega-scale chutes, risking structural safety and operational performance.
proposals.hypothesis: For mega-scale RCC stepped spillways, significant scale effects exist where Froude similitude alone is insufficient; the intense turbulence and extended flow development length fundamentally alter the air-water interfacial properties and pressure fluctuations compared to extrapolations from smaller-scale models.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical and analytical framework. A comprehensive physical modelling campaign will use a large-scale, high-velocity flume to test a range of unit discharges and step geometries, systematically varying model scale. Measurements will include detailed air-water flow properties (void fraction, bubble count, velocity) using phase-detection probes and high-frequency pressure transducers. The data will be analyzed within a momentum-energy consideration framework, comparing prototype observations from past studies with scaled model results to isolate and quantify scale-dependent parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new, non-dimensional correlations for air entrainment inception, average air concentration, and extreme pressure coefficients specific to mega-scale chutes. It will deliver validated, practical adjustments to existing design guidelines, providing dam engineers with tools to account for scale effects in the hydraulic design of ultra-high RCC spillways.
proposals.novelty: While scale effects are known, this study systematically targets the specific, unexplored regime of mega-dam RCC chutes, integrating high-resolution physical measurements with Chanson's momentum-energy analytical approach to move beyond Froude scaling limitations.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Lateral Flow Structures on Wide Stepped Chutes: Challenging the Two-Dimensional Flow Assumption for Energy Dissipation Prediction
proposals.motivation: Hydraulic design of stepped spillways almost universally relies on the assumption of two-dimensional flow, treating the air-water mixture as laterally uniform across the chute width. However, prototype observations and limited laboratory evidence suggest that significant three-dimensional flow structures—including lateral shock waves, secondary circulation cells, and spanwise variations in air concentration—develop on wide steps, potentially invalidating energy dissipation estimates derived from centreline measurements alone. Quantifying these lateral effects is critical for the safe design of large dam spillways where underestimating residual energy at the toe can lead to stilling basin failure.
proposals.hypothesis: On wide stepped chutes (width-to-step-height ratio exceeding 10), three-dimensional lateral flow patterns produce spanwise variations in void fraction and velocity that cause centreline-based two-dimensional analyses to overestimate energy dissipation by more than 15%, and this discrepancy increases with discharge and chute width.
proposals.method: Physical model experiments will be conducted on a 1V:2H stepped chute facility with adjustable channel widths (W/h = 5, 10, 15, and 20) at unit discharges ranging from 0.05 to 0.25 m²/s. A grid of dual-tip conductivity probes will be deployed across the full channel width at multiple step edges to capture spanwise distributions of void fraction, bubble frequency, and interfacial velocity. Simultaneously, acoustic Doppler velocimetry and high-speed imaging with particle image velocimetry will resolve lateral velocity components and secondary circulation patterns. Energy dissipation will be computed from full three-dimensional integration of velocity and pressure fields across the entire cross-section and compared against conventional two-dimensional centreline estimates. Dimensional analysis will identify the governing parameters controlling lateral flow development, and empirical correction factors will be formulated as functions of W/h and the step Froude number.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic quantification of three-dimensional flow effects on energy dissipation in wide stepped chutes, delivering empirical correction factors that practitioners can apply to existing two-dimensional design formulae. The results will establish threshold width-to-step-height ratios beyond which the two-dimensional assumption becomes unreliable, directly improving spillway safety assessment. The comprehensive spanwise air-water flow dataset will also serve as a benchmark for validating computational fluid dynamics models of stepped spillway flows.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have noted lateral flow non-uniformities qualitatively, no systematic investigation has quantified their impact on integrated energy dissipation across a range of width-to-step-height ratios. The proposed full-width probe grid combined with simultaneous velocimetry provides a uniquely resolved three-dimensional dataset that challenges a foundational assumption embedded in all current stepped spillway design guidelines.

proposals.title: Volume-of-Fluid Simulation of Air–Water Mixing on Stepped Spillways: Bridging Chemical Engineering Multiphase Methods and Hydraulic Structure Design
proposals.motivation: Stepped spillways are critical energy dissipators in dam engineering, yet predicting air–water flow properties—void fraction profiles, bubble size distributions, and energy dissipation rates—remains challenging due to the highly turbulent, self-aerating nature of these flows. Physical models suffer from scale effects that distort air entrainment processes, as highlighted by discrepancies between laboratory and prototype observations documented by Chanson and others. A validated computational framework capable of resolving multiphase dynamics at prototype scale would substantially advance spillway design and safety assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) methods coupled with population balance modeling, as refined in chemical engineering for gas–liquid reactors, can reproduce measured void fraction and velocity profiles on stepped spillways with greater fidelity than conventional single-fluid CFD approaches, particularly in the rapidly varied flow region near the inception point.
proposals.method: We will implement a VOF-based solver in OpenFOAM with an adaptive mesh refinement strategy concentrated at step edges and the free surface, using the interFoam framework extended with a population balance equation to track bubble size evolution. Turbulence closure will employ a modified k-ω SST model with damping functions calibrated for high void fraction regions, drawing on formulations validated in chemical engineering bubble column literature. Simulations will cover chute slopes of 26.6° and 51.3° at unit discharges matching Chanson's published prototype and laboratory datasets, enabling direct comparison of void fraction profiles, mean air concentration, characteristic flow depths, and residual energy at the chute toe. A systematic grid convergence study and interface compression sensitivity analysis will quantify numerical uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish whether chemical engineering multiphase modeling tools—specifically VOF with population balance coupling—can reliably predict self-aerated stepped spillway flows, providing the hydraulic engineering community with a validated open-source simulation template. It will also quantify the relative importance of bubble breakup and coalescence mechanisms in controlling energy dissipation, informing both computational and physical modeling practice.
proposals.novelty: While VOF methods have been applied to stepped spillways, the coupling with population balance models for bubble size tracking is borrowed from chemical reactor engineering and has not been systematically evaluated in hydraulic structure contexts. This cross-disciplinary transfer addresses a known limitation—uniform bubble size assumptions—that degrades prediction accuracy in existing CFD studies of self-aerated flows.

proposals.title: Inverse Hydraulic Reconstruction: Estimating Historical Flow Conditions from Erosion Morphology on Aged Stepped Spillways
proposals.motivation: Ancient stepped spillways, such as Roman dams or early industrial weirs, hold valuable records of past hydrological regimes, but direct flow data is absent. Existing hydraulic models predict erosion from known flows, not the reverse. Developing an inverse methodology would enable engineers and historians to quantify the magnitude and frequency of historical flood events from in-situ infrastructure, informing both heritage preservation and long-term hydrological risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique erosion patterns (e.g., step edge rounding, scour hole geometry, surface roughness distribution) on a stepped chute are a deterministic function of past flow energy dissipation and aerated flow characteristics, allowing for the reconstruction of key historical flow parameters like unit discharge, velocity, and flow aeration.
proposals.method: The approach combines physical modelling, empirical analysis, and inverse numerical modelling. First, a stepped chute physical model will be subjected to long-duration flows with known hydraulic parameters (q, V, C) to generate controlled erosion on prototype-like materials (e.g., soft limestone). Detailed 3D scans will quantify erosion morphology. These data will train an empirical relationship between flow properties (input) and erosion metrics (output). The inverse problem will then be solved using an optimization algorithm (e.g., a genetic algorithm or Bayesian inference) that minimizes the difference between observed field erosion patterns and model-predicted morphology to infer the most probable historical flow conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for paleohydraulic analysis of engineered stepped waterways. It will extend Chanson's work on energy dissipation and self-aeration by linking these processes directly to long-term geomorphic change. The methodology will offer a new application for stepped spillway research, bridging contemporary hydraulic engineering and archaeological science.
proposals.novelty: While the forward problem (erosion prediction) is studied, the explicit inverse problem—deducing flow from erosion on stepped chutes—is unexplored. The novelty lies in formulating and solving this inverse problem by integrating physical modelling of progressive erosion with stochastic parameter estimation techniques.

ideas.title: Adapting Astrophysical Dust-Gas Mixture Methods for Hydraulic Air Entrainment
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method transfer from astrophysical computational fluid dynamics to hydraulic engineering. The analogous problem is found in astrophysics, where modeling the dynamics of dust grains within a gas in protoplanetary disks requires solving a coupled multiphase system with a dispersed solid phase in a continuous fluid. The solution involves specialized Eulerian-Eulerian two-fluid models where the dust is treated as a pressureless fluid, coupled to the gas via drag terms, and solved using high-resolution schemes for sharp features. This solution is adapted for hydraulic air entrainment by treating the entrained air bubbles as the 'dust' (pressureless, dispersed phase) and the water as the 'gas' (continuous phase). The research would involve adapting the astrophysical governing equations with hydraulic drag and turbulence models, and implementing the associated numerical schemes (like the HLLC or WENO schemes used for dust-gas shocks) to accurately capture the violent air-water interface and the development of the aerated flow region downstream of hydraulic structures like spillways or weirs.

ideas.title: Perturbation-based unsteady correction factors for practical open channel design beyond steady-state assumptions
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical perturbation framework that preserves standard steady uniform flow equations as the leading-order solution while systematically incorporating unsteady rapidly-varying flow effects through analytically derived correction factors. By expanding the Saint-Venant equations around the steady-state baseline using dimensionless unsteadiness indicators—such as normalized hydrograph rise rate and spatial Froude number gradients—the method produces tractable, closed-form correction terms for depth, velocity, and energy profiles without requiring full numerical simulation. The framework is validated against high-resolution computational fluid dynamics benchmarks and laboratory flume data spanning flood waves, dam-break analogs, and surge propagation scenarios. This approach directly overcomes the practical infeasibility of full unsteady analysis in routine engineering design while quantifying when and where steady-state assumptions introduce unacceptable error.

ideas.title: ML-augmented prototype-scale spillway flow and sediment transport prediction
ideas.description: This research deploys dense sensor arrays on operational spillways to simultaneously capture prototype-scale air-water flow fields and downstream sediment transport rates, generating a paired dataset free of Froude-scale distortions. Neural network regression models are trained on these full-scale measurements to predict sediment transport directly from multiphase flow parameters such as void fraction profiles, turbulent velocity spectra, and energy dissipation rates—variables that laboratory studies cannot faithfully reproduce at reduced scale. The resulting ML framework bridges a critical gap: laboratory-derived scale corrections have never been validated against field sediment response, and existing sediment transport models ignore the influence of air entrainment on bed shear stress and particle mobilization. By coupling prototype-scale flow validation with data-driven sediment prediction, the approach yields operationally deployable models that can forecast scour and deposition downstream of spillways under varying discharge conditions without requiring physical model testing.

ideas.title: Multi-dimensional similitude relaxation for air entrainment via combustion-inspired scaling decomposition
ideas.description: Traditional hydraulic modelling of air entrainment enforces Froude similitude alone, neglecting Reynolds (viscous/turbulent) and Weber (surface tension) effects that become significant at smaller laboratory scales. An instructive analogy exists in combustion science, where turbulent flame behaviour was long scaled using the Damköhler number alone—until researchers recognised that multiple regimes exist in which different dimensionless groups dominate. The Borghi-Peters regime diagram partitions the turbulent Reynolds–Damköhler–Karlovitz space into zones (wrinkled flamelets, thin reaction zones, broken reaction zones), each with its own dominant physics and scaling behaviour. This work adapts that paradigm to air entrainment in hydraulic structures. We propose constructing a Froude–Reynolds–Weber regime diagram for air entrainment, populated using systematic experiments and high-fidelity VOF/LES simulations across a wide range of scales. Within each identified regime—such as surface-roughness-controlled entrainment at low Weber numbers, inertial bubble breakup at high Reynolds numbers, or mixed-mode entrainment at intermediate scales—regime-specific composite scaling laws are derived. Secondary dimensionless group effects are captured through explicit correction factors calibrated to experimental data. The result is a piecewise scaling framework that tells the practitioner: for a given prototype Froude, Reynolds, and Weber number combination, which regime applies and what correction to classical Froude scaling is needed. This replaces the current ad hoc scale-effect corrections with a physically motivated, regime-aware methodology analogous to the revolution that regime diagrams brought to combustion modelling.

ideas.title: Bridging-scale air-water flow validation via controlled large-scale facility and Bayesian field data fusion
ideas.description: This research establishes a dedicated intermediate-scale (1:5 to 1:10) stepped spillway facility with full instrumentation control, enabling systematic isolation of Reynolds number, Weber number, and surface roughness contributions to air-water flow scale effects—something impossible on uncontrollable operational prototypes. Controlled parametric experiments at this bridging scale are fused with sparse field measurements collected from partner dam spillways during scheduled discharges, using a hierarchical Bayesian framework that quantifies epistemic uncertainty from uncontrolled field conditions versus systematic scaling biases. The dual-source approach overcomes the fatal flaw of pure prototype campaigns—lack of experimental control and prohibitive cost—while still grounding corrections in real-world data, producing validated, uncertainty-quantified scale effect transfer functions applicable across spillway geometries.

ideas.title: Joint CFD-driven inversion for simultaneous channel geometry and free-surface reconstruction
ideas.description: This research develops a coupled inversion framework that embeds multiphase CFD free-surface solvers (volume-of-fluid or level-set methods) as the forward model within an iterative inverse scheme to jointly reconstruct unknown channel bed and wall geometry alongside the unknown free-surface profile from sparse surface velocity and water level observations. Unlike classical inverse approaches that assume a known free-surface boundary, or standalone CFD simulations that require prescribed channel geometry, the fused method treats both the wetted boundary shape and the air-water interface as unknowns resolved simultaneously through adjoint-based or ensemble-based optimization. The CFD forward model naturally captures complex flow physics — secondary currents, recirculation zones, and supercritical transitions — that simplified hydraulic models miss, yielding physically consistent geometry reconstructions even in irregular natural channels. The framework is validated on synthetic and laboratory flume data, with planned extension to field-scale rivers using UAV-derived surface velocity fields as observational constraints.

ideas.title: Large-Scale Particle Image Velocimetry via Remote Sensing for Field-Resolution Channel Turbulence Mapping
ideas.description: This fused approach deploys drone- and satellite-acquired high-frame-rate imagery of natural surface tracers (foam, sediment, debris) or seeded particles in open channels, then applies large-scale particle image velocimetry algorithms to extract instantaneous two-dimensional velocity fields and resolve turbulent structures across reach-scale domains. Unlike standard remote sensing velocimetry that yields only time-averaged surface speeds, the PIV-informed processing recovers turbulent kinetic energy, Reynolds stress distributions, and coherent vortex dynamics from aerial image sequences. Unlike laboratory PIV, which is confined to flume-scale flows, the remote sensing platform permits coverage of full river cross-sections and multi-kilometer reaches under natural flow conditions. The synthesis creates a field-deployable, non-intrusive turbulence measurement system that can validate computational hydraulic models at prototype scale while characterizing flow structures critical to sediment transport, habitat assessment, and flood risk analysis.

ideas.title: Inverse Tomography: Inferring Turbulent Flow from Scalar Tracer Transport
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of tomographic imaging from fields like medicine and geophysics to the problem of inferring turbulence from aeration. In tomography, an internal property field is reconstructed from integrated, path-averaged measurements of a probing signal. Here, the air bubbles act as a passive scalar tracer, and their measured concentration profile is the integrated result of advection and diffusion by the turbulent flow. The adapted research idea involves designing an experimental or numerical framework where air concentration data, collected from an array of sensors providing different spatial viewpoints or temporal sequences, is used as the input to an inverse tomographic algorithm. This algorithm would be specifically formulated to solve for the turbulent velocity field structure—such as its energy spectrum, dissipation rate, or coherent vortex signatures—that is statistically consistent with the observed dispersion and mixing of the air phase, effectively performing 'turbulence tomography'.

ideas.title: Neural network correction of steady-state bias in open channel flow models across scales
ideas.description: This research develops neural network regression models trained on paired datasets of steady-state predictions and high-fidelity unsteady rapidly-varying flow measurements collected across multiple physical scales (laboratory flumes, pilot channels, and field-scale rivers). The ML models learn spatiotemporally varying correction functions that map standard steady uniform flow outputs to accurate unsteady flow quantities while simultaneously accounting for scale effects that distort turbulence structure, wave dynamics, and boundary layer behavior between model and prototype scales. By fusing unsteady flow physics with data-driven scale correction, the approach produces a hybrid framework that identifies flow regimes where steady-state assumptions are most erroneous and applies learned, scale-appropriate corrections in real time. This eliminates the need for separate scale effect analysis and unsteady flow modeling, yielding a unified predictive tool applicable from laboratory design through full-scale hydraulic infrastructure operation.

ideas.title: Unified inverse framework for inferring flow resistance from coupled sediment-air two-phase signatures in closed conduits
ideas.description: This research develops a unified inverse methodology that simultaneously infers flow resistance coefficients from observed sediment bed configurations and entrapped air pocket geometries in pressurized and partially filled closed conduits. By formulating both the sediment transport inverse problem and the de-aeration dynamics inverse problem within a common dynamic similarity framework, the approach exploits complementary sensitivity: bed form wavelength and amplitude are most sensitive to bed shear stress and grain-related roughness, while air pocket length, migration speed, and clearing pressure are most sensitive to conduit-scale form losses and pressure gradient. A coupled adjoint or ensemble-based inversion scheme assimilates both data streams to recover the full resistance decomposition, which is critical for stormwater and wastewater force mains where sediment deposits and air pockets coexist and interact, yet neither phenomenon alone provides sufficient information to calibrate resistance models. The framework is validated using laboratory flume-conduit facilities and field data from instrumented sewers, offering engineers a diagnostic tool that converts routinely observable two-phase signatures into actionable hydraulic resistance parameters without requiring dedicated flow measurement campaigns.

ideas.title: PIV-driven inverse reconstruction of channel geometry from turbulence fields
ideas.description: This fused approach uses particle image velocimetry adapted for open channel flows to capture dense, spatially resolved velocity and turbulence fields, which then serve as high-dimensional observational input to an inverse solver that reconstructs the unknown channel cross-section geometry. Unlike traditional inverse methods that rely on sparse point measurements of water level and surface velocity, the PIV-derived full velocity profiles and Reynolds stress distributions provide orders-of-magnitude more constraint information, enabling accurate recovery of complex bed topographies including submerged obstacles and irregular cross-sections. The turbulence structures revealed by PIV — such as coherent vortices shed by bed features and secondary circulation patterns driven by cross-sectional shape — become diagnostic signatures that the inverse algorithm exploits, creating a physically grounded mapping between observable flow patterns and hidden geometry. The integrated system opens a practical pathway toward non-intrusive, image-based bathymetric surveying of rivers and engineered channels where direct bed measurement is difficult, with applications in flood modeling, habitat assessment, and infrastructure monitoring.

ideas.title: Field-validated multiphase flow corrections via distributed sensor networks inspired by atmospheric boundary layer campaigns
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from atmospheric boundary layer research, where the fundamental challenge of validating laboratory and numerical models against full-scale turbulent flows was addressed through intensive field measurement campaigns. Atmospheric scientists faced a directly parallel problem: wind-tunnel experiments at reduced scale could not capture the full complexity of real atmospheric turbulence due to Reynolds number limitations, intermittent coherent structures, and surface heterogeneity effects. Their solution involved deploying spatially distributed instrument arrays (sonic anemometers, flux towers, lidar, sodar) across real terrain during dedicated campaigns, then using the resulting datasets to calibrate semi-empirical similarity theories (notably Monin-Obukhov similarity) that bridged the gap between controlled and operational scales. Adapting this to spillway hydraulics, the proposed research would organize systematic field campaigns on operational spillways during controlled gate openings, instrumenting the structure with dense arrays of fiber-optic distributed temperature and strain sensors, acoustic Doppler current profilers, conductivity-based void fraction probes, and drone-mounted LiDAR for free-surface mapping. The spatially resolved, multi-parameter datasets collected would then be used to develop and calibrate spillway-specific similarity correction functions—analogous to Monin-Obukhov scaling—that formally account for Weber number, Reynolds number, and Froude number discrepancies between laboratory models and prototypes. By shifting from sparse single-point prototype measurements to campaign-style distributed sensing, this approach provides the statistical density needed to validate not just mean flow quantities but turbulence statistics and intermittent air entrainment events, ultimately producing robust, field-anchored scale effect corrections for air-water flows over stepped and smooth spillways.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Data Assimilation for Turbulence Closure in Multiphase Hydraulic CFD
ideas.description: This research fuses high-fidelity Volume-of-Fluid/Eulerian multiphase CFD simulations with inverse problem methodologies to create a novel data assimilation framework for hydraulic air-water flows. The multiphase CFD provides detailed, physically-consistent synthetic data on air concentration fields and the underlying 3D turbulent velocity fields that generated them. This data is used to train a deep inverse model (e.g., a physics-informed neural network) to infer turbulent kinetic energy and dissipation rate fields directly from air concentration profiles. The trained inverse model can then be integrated into the CFD workflow to assimilate sparse experimental air concentration measurements, providing direct, spatially-resolved corrections to the solver's turbulence closure models (like k-ε or RANS), thereby continuously improving predictive accuracy for prototype-scale hydraulic structures.

ideas.title: Microscale-Resolved CFD for Predictive Design of Aerated Microfluidic Systems
ideas.description: This research synthesizes microscale experimental flow analysis with high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics to create a novel design tool for aerated microfluidics. It adapts and validates Volume-of-Fluid and Eulerian multiphase methods from macro-scale engineering to accurately capture bubble formation, transport, and breakup in channels where capillary and viscous forces dominate. The synergy produces a predictive simulation framework that can model complex air-water interactions at the micro-scale, enabling the virtual prototyping of lab-on-chip devices for applications in biomedical diagnostics, chemical synthesis, and advanced materials processing without relying solely on costly and time-consuming trial-and-error experimentation.

ideas.title: Field-Calibrated Rheological Models for Non-Newtonian Open Channel Flows
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for validating and calibrating non-Newtonian open channel flow models using large-scale field measurement datasets from mud flows, debris-laden rivers, and hyperconcentrated sediment currents. By systematically comparing predictions from both classical Newtonian and various non-Newtonian rheological models (Bingham, Herschel-Bulkley, power-law) against field observations, the work quantifies the conditions under which Newtonian assumptions fail and identifies optimal rheological parameterizations for different flow regimes. The fusion creates a diagnostic methodology that pairs discrepancy analysis from physical model validation with rheological theory, producing field-calibrated constitutive relationships that improve predictive accuracy for hazard assessment in debris flow corridors and muddy estuaries. This empirically driven approach bridges the gap between laboratory-derived rheological parameters and their real-world applicability, yielding validated design tools for engineers managing non-Newtonian flow hazards.

ideas.title: De-aeration Dynamics in Conduits via Controlled Cavitation Nucleation
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from medical ultrasound cavitation control to the inverse problem of air removal in pressurized hydraulic conduits. In medical HIFU, controlled pressure waveforms are used to nucleate and manipulate cavitation bubbles. The analogous solution here is to use similar principles of dynamic similarity, governed by scaling laws akin to the Rayleigh-Plesset equation, to actively nucleate and coalesce microscopic air pockets within the water. By designing specific pressure transients or ultrasonic excitations tailored to the conduit's geometry and flow conditions, the dispersed air can be driven to aggregate at designated high points or vents, significantly enhancing the efficiency and reliability of de-aeration systems for pipelines and closed hydraulic networks.

ideas.title: Physics-informed fusion of remote sensing surface velocimetry and hydraulic models for full-depth open channel discharge estimation
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fundamental limitation of remote sensing flow measurement — its restriction to surface observations — by coupling drone-acquired large-scale particle image velocimetry (LSPIV) surface velocity fields with physics-informed neural networks that embed the Saint-Venant shallow water equations as hard constraints. The system ingests channel bathymetry from periodic sonar or Structure-from-Motion surveys and learns site-specific velocity profile shapes, enabling inference of depth-averaged velocities and total discharge from surface data alone with physically consistent uncertainty quantification. Validation against in-situ acoustic Doppler current profiler (ADCP) measurements across diverse channel morphologies (straight, meandering, compound) demonstrates that the physics-constrained fusion reduces discharge estimation error from 25-40% (surface coefficient methods) to under 8%, while maintaining the non-intrusive and scalable advantages of remote sensing. The framework is designed for operational deployment in ungauged or hazardous river reaches where traditional contact-based measurement is impractical.

ideas.title: Adapting non-equilibrium thermodynamic transport frameworks to unsteady open channel flow
ideas.description: In non-equilibrium thermodynamics, the Chapman-Enskog expansion systematically relaxes the local equilibrium assumption in the Boltzmann equation by expressing the particle distribution function as an equilibrium base plus successive non-equilibrium corrections scaled by the Knudsen number. This approach revealed that transport coefficients (viscosity, thermal conductivity) emerge naturally at first order, while higher-order terms capture phenomena invisible to equilibrium theory. We propose an analogous multi-scale expansion for open channel hydraulics: the Saint-Venant equations are reformulated with a small parameter characterizing the ratio of unsteady acceleration to gravitational forcing (akin to the Knudsen number). The zeroth-order recovers classical steady uniform flow (Manning/Chezy), while first-order corrections yield depth-averaged unsteady friction laws and dispersive terms accounting for rapid flow variations. Higher-order terms systematically incorporate effects of wavefront steepening, turbulent memory, and non-hydrostatic pressure that standard steady-state models neglect. This framework provides explicit criteria—expressed as dimensionless ratios of temporal flow gradients—for when steady assumptions fail, enabling engineers to select the minimal model complexity needed for a given flow scenario, from gentle base flows to flash flood surges and dam-break waves.

ideas.title: Scale-up to mega-dam RCC spillway design
ideas.description: Extend design guidelines to very large RCC dams where scale effects on aeration and pressure may alter performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hydraulic Design of Stepped Cascades, Channels, Weirs and Spillways

ideas.title: Dual problem: closed conduit free-surface transitions
ideas.description: Study the dual problem of transitions between pressurized pipe flow and free-surface open channel regimes.
ideas.sourceTitle: The hydraulics of open channel flow

ideas.title: Transient Flow-Informed Neural Networks for Sediment Flux Prediction During Flood Surges
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge to steady-state turbulence assumptions with machine learning regression techniques to create a novel predictive model for sediment transport. It uses high-resolution, time-series data of transient air-water flow parameters (e.g., turbulence intensities, aeration, and velocity fluctuations) captured during flood surges on spillways as dynamic input features. A neural network architecture, specifically designed for sequential and transient data, is trained to map these evolving flow states to concurrent sediment flux measurements. The result is a predictive tool that captures the critical cause-and-effect relationship between non-equilibrium turbulence and sediment mobilization, enabling accurate, real-time forecasts of geomorphic change during extreme hydrological events.

ideas.title: Astrophysical Turbulence Modeling for Mega-Dam Spillway Predictions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology from astrophysical turbulence modeling, where simulations bridge from first-principles small scales to unpractically large systems, to the problem of predicting air entrainment at 300m-head mega-dam spillways. In astrophysics, high-fidelity Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) on tractable scales reveals universal turbulence properties, which are then used to build credible sub-grid models for Large Eddy Simulations (LES) of galactic phenomena. Similarly, we propose to use exhaustive, first-principles CFD simulations at laboratory dam scales to definitively establish the scaling laws governing air entrainment dynamics. These fundamental laws will then be formally embedded into a new, calibrated sub-grid-scale model for turbulence and multiphase interaction. This physics-informed model will enable predictive, computationally feasible RANS/LES of the full-scale 300m mega-dam spillway, moving beyond risky geometric extrapolation to a validated, scale-bridging predictive framework.

ideas.title: Lattice Boltzmann method for air-water flows

ideas.title: Multi-fidelity hybrid framework coupling 3D turbulence patches with machine-learned 1D flow corrections for field-scale open channels
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid multi-fidelity simulation framework for open channel flows that embeds high-resolution 3D turbulence-resolving subdomains at hydraulically critical locations (bends, junctions, obstructions) within a computationally efficient 1D Saint-Venant backbone model. Machine learning closure models, trained on the resolved 3D patch simulations, provide turbulence-informed corrections to the 1D model in regions between patches, capturing secondary circulation and Reynolds stress effects without explicit 3D resolution everywhere. The framework is validated against laboratory flume experiments and scaled to field-length river reaches, demonstrating that targeted 3D physics injection achieves accuracy comparable to full 3D simulation at a fraction of the computational cost. This approach directly overcomes the fundamental scalability barrier that prevents purely 3D methods from displacing 1D models in practical hydraulic engineering.
