ideas.title: From cardiac flow to open channels: adopting medical imaging-coupled CFD for fully 3D turbulent channel hydraulics
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between the historical transition in cardiovascular biomechanics—from 1D lumped-parameter and Womersley-type tube models to patient-specific 3D turbulence-resolving CFD—and the current challenge in open channel hydraulics, where 1D Saint-Venant equations remain dominant despite known failures at bends, confluences, compound cross-sections, and vegetated reaches. In hemodynamics, the breakthrough came from combining medical imaging (CT/MRI) with LES and DNS on patient-specific geometries, validated against 4D flow MRI velocity fields, and made computationally tractable through multi-scale coupling where 1D arterial network models supplied boundary conditions to localized 3D domains of clinical interest. Adapting this approach, the proposed research would: (1) use high-resolution channel bathymetry from LiDAR or Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry as the geometric input analogous to medical imaging; (2) embed 3D LES/DNS subdomains at hydraulically complex locations (meander bends, bridge piers, confluences, weirs) within a 1D or 2D shallow-water backbone model using domain decomposition with overlapping or matched interfaces; (3) validate resolved turbulent structures against field measurements from acoustic Doppler current profilers and large-scale particle image velocimetry, serving as the open-channel equivalent of 4D flow MRI; and (4) leverage immersed boundary methods and adaptive mesh refinement techniques originally refined for vascular geometries to handle irregular bed and bank topography. This multi-scale framework would preserve computational efficiency for reach-scale applications while capturing the three-dimensional turbulent phenomena—secondary currents, coherent vortex structures, separation zones—that 1D models cannot represent, ultimately improving predictions of sediment transport, erosion, pollutant mixing, and flood risk at critical channel features.

ideas.title: Chord length distributions for bubble shape

ideas.description: Scale down stepped channel geometry to microfluidic devices for enhanced mixing and gas transfer in lab-on-chip systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hydraulic Design of Stepped Cascades, Channels, Weirs and Spillways

ideas.title: Multi-scale empirical similitude framework for aerator jets with learned scale-effect corrections
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid similitude framework for aerator jet devices that moves beyond classical Froude-Weber matching by conducting coordinated experiments across three or more geometric scales to explicitly measure scale-dependent deviations caused by Reynolds effects, bubble population dynamics, and surface contamination. A machine-learning model is trained on the multi-scale dataset to learn continuous correction functions that map reduced-scale measurements to prototype-scale performance, with uncertainty quantification bounding the extrapolation error. The framework is validated by withholding the largest-scale data during training and demonstrating predictive accuracy for air entrainment rate, bubble size distribution, and downstream dissolved oxygen within specified tolerance bands. This approach directly overcomes the fundamental incompleteness of two-parameter similitude by empirically capturing the influence of all non-matched dimensionless groups through data rather than assuming they are negligible.

ideas.title: Ultrasonic rheological profiling of non-Newtonian open channel flows via entrained bubble dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops an ultrasonic bubble-tracking framework that repurposes medical imaging techniques to characterize the rheology of non-Newtonian fluids in open channel settings such as mud flows, debris-laden currents, and slurry transport. By injecting or leveraging naturally entrained microbubbles as passive rheological probes, high-frequency ultrasound arrays capture bubble shape deformation, oscillation damping, and migration patterns that encode the local shear-thinning, viscoelastic, or yield-stress behavior of the carrier fluid. Inverse models linking bubble dynamics to constitutive parameters (e.g., Herschel-Bulkley or Bingham models) enable spatially resolved, real-time rheological mapping across the channel cross-section without physical sampling or flow disruption. The fused approach uniquely bridges a critical gap: non-Newtonian open channel theory currently lacks in-situ validation tools, while medical ultrasound bubble detection has never been applied to extract rheological information in complex environmental and industrial flows.

ideas.title: Adaptive-Rheology Open Channel Framework for Natural Debris Flows via Real-Time Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research develops an open channel hydraulics framework for non-Newtonian mud and debris flows that overcomes the fundamental limitation of unknown and spatially varying rheological parameters. The system couples depth-averaged shallow-water equations with a physics-informed neural network that continuously infers local Herschel-Bulkley constitutive parameters from readily observable field proxies—surface velocity imagery, basal pressure measurements, and seismic ground vibration signatures. The neural network is pre-trained on extensive laboratory flume experiments spanning diverse sediment concentrations and grain size distributions, then fine-tuned via ensemble Kalman filter data assimilation during live events. This adaptive approach enables accurate flow depth, velocity, and runout predictions even as debris composition evolves along the channel, providing a practical advance over static-parameter models for hazard forecasting.

ideas.title: Questioning probe non-intrusiveness at high void
ideas.description: Investigate how intrusive probes alter the flow structure they measure, especially at void fractions above 30%.
ideas.sourceTitle: Air-Water Flow Measurements with Intrusive, Phase-Detection Probes: Can We Improve Their Interpretation?

ideas.title: Tomographic PIV with adaptive surface-distortion correction for open channel turbulence characterization
ideas.description: This system advances open channel turbulence measurement by deploying volumetric tomographic PIV with fluorescent tracers and narrowband optical filters to reject ambient light contamination inherent to hydraulic environments. A synchronized structured-light profilometer continuously maps the free-surface geometry, enabling real-time ray-tracing corrections that eliminate refractive distortions of the illumination volume caused by surface waves. A convolutional neural network preprocessing stage classifies and removes spurious signals from sediment particles, air bubbles, and surface reflections, preserving only valid tracer data for velocity reconstruction. Together, these innovations overcome the three principal barriers—optical access, seeding purity, and surface distortion—that have prevented reliable transfer of high-resolution PIV from aerodynamic wind tunnels to turbulent open channel flows.

ideas.title: Operator-splitting and partitioned coupling strategies adapted from fluid-structure interaction for sediment-flow modeling
ideas.description: Coupled sediment-flow modeling shares a fundamental structure with fluid-structure interaction (FSI) in computational aeroelasticity: two physics domains are tightly coupled through a deforming interface—in FSI the wetted structural surface, in morphodynamics the evolving channel bed. The FSI community solved this by developing partitioned coupling frameworks where independent, optimized solvers for fluid and structural mechanics exchange interface data iteratively within each timestep, using convergence acceleration techniques such as Aitken relaxation and interface quasi-Newton methods (IQN-ILS). These approaches avoid monolithic reformulation, preserve modularity, and handle the numerical instability caused by strong two-way coupling (the added-mass instability analog). Adapting this to sediment-flow modeling, one would partition the problem into a hydrodynamic solver (solving shallow water or 3D Navier-Stokes equations with free-surface tracking) and a morphodynamic solver (solving the Exner equation coupled with bedload and suspended sediment transport). At each timestep, the hydrodynamic solver provides bed shear stress and near-bed velocity to the morphodynamic solver, which returns updated bed geometry and roughness. Quasi-Newton coupling with subspace acceleration ensures convergence even in strongly-coupled regimes such as rapid bed aggradation, dune migration, or localized scour around bridge piers. This partitioned strategy enables researchers to leverage existing high-fidelity flow solvers (e.g., OpenFOAM, TELEMAC) and sediment codes (e.g., SISYPHE, SRH-2D) without rewriting either, while achieving coupling robustness comparable to monolithic approaches.

ideas.title: Variable roughness and dynamic friction coefficients

ideas.sourceTitle: The hydraulics of open channel flow

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Subsurface Imaging for Bubble Cloud Characterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from geophysical seismic imaging to the original problem of non-intrusive bubble measurement in hydraulic flows. In seismology, arrays of sensors and advanced computational techniques like full-waveform inversion are used to create detailed 3D images of subsurface structures from reflected sound waves. The adapted research idea applies this principle by deploying an array of ultrasonic transducers around a flow conduit and using seismic-style full-waveform inversion algorithms on the recorded backscatter data. This processing would solve the inverse acoustic problem to map not just individual bubble sizes, but the full 3D distribution and morphology of bubble clouds within the turbulent flow, analogous to how seismologists map complex geological formations.

ideas.title: Adapting optical flow cytometry tracking principles for open channel turbulence characterization
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy between the challenge of characterizing turbulent structures in open channel flows using PIV and the biomedical engineering problem of tracking heterogeneous cell populations in imaging flow cytometry. In flow cytometry, dense suspensions of particles (cells) are optically interrogated using multi-channel laser systems and high-speed cameras, with cross-correlation algorithms and machine learning classifiers used to resolve individual trajectories and classify subpopulations despite overlapping signals. Adapting this approach, the proposed method introduces spectrally distinct fluorescent tracer particles of varying sizes into open channel flows, each size class resonating with different turbulent length scales. A multi-wavelength PIV imaging system—inspired by multi-channel cytometric detection—simultaneously captures the motion of each tracer class. Cytometry-derived cross-correlation and classification algorithms are then applied to decompose the velocity fields by scale, enabling simultaneous characterization of large-scale coherent structures (sweeps, ejections, and secondary currents) and small-scale dissipative eddies within a single experimental run. This multi-scale, multi-tracer PIV framework overcomes the traditional trade-off between spatial resolution and field of view in conventional single-tracer PIV, offering a richer characterization of turbulence in open channel hydraulics.

ideas.title: Self-calibrating continental river network models via bed form-inverted roughness fields
ideas.description: This research develops a continental-scale river network hydraulic modeling framework that self-calibrates its flow resistance parameters by inverting observed sediment bed form configurations across the entire network. Rather than relying on uniform Manning's n values or sparse gauge-based calibration, the approach uses remotely sensed and in-situ bed form data (dune heights, ripple wavelengths, grain size distributions) at each reach to solve the dual problem of estimating local roughness coefficients, which then propagate through the network's coupled mass and momentum equations governing tributary confluences and floodplain storage exchange. The fusion creates a feedback loop unavailable to either idea alone: network-scale flow routing constrains the hydraulic context (depth, velocity, slope) needed to interpret bed forms, while the bed form inversion supplies the spatially heterogeneous resistance fields that network models desperately need but cannot otherwise obtain at continental scale. This coupled framework promises substantially improved flood wave celerity predictions, backwater effect modeling at confluences, and ungauged basin discharge estimation across diverse geomorphic settings.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Adjoint Assimilation of Turbulence from Aeration
ideas.description: This redesigned system tackles the fundamental non-uniqueness of the inverse problem by embedding fluid dynamics laws directly into the inference process. It employs an adjoint method to efficiently compute gradients of a loss function that penalizes mismatch with measured air concentration data and deviation from the Navier-Stokes equations. The core innovation is a differentiable numerical solver that allows the turbulent velocity field to be iteratively optimized, ensuring the final reconstruction is both data-consistent and physically plausible. This results in a uniquely constrained and dynamically coherent estimate of the underlying turbulence structure.

ideas.title: Pipe-Integrated Ultrasonic Array with Model-Based Reverberation Cancellation for Bubble Characterization
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs a custom ultrasonic transducer array, optimized for the specific pipe material and operating at lower frequencies (e.g., 500 kHz - 2 MHz) to penetrate metallic walls, mounted via an impedance-matching wedge. A real-time digital signal processing core runs a physics-based model of the multi-layer system (wedge, pipe wall, fluid) to predict and subtract the deterministic reverberation clutter. The cleansed signal is then analyzed using inverse scattering techniques to resolve the size distribution and spatial density of bubbles within the turbulent flow, providing robust, non-intrusive measurement in industrial hydraulic systems.

ideas.title: Vascular network modeling applied to continental river hydraulics
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between continental river network hydraulics and computational hemodynamics, where the challenge of simulating flow through a vast branching vascular tree mirrors the problem of scaling single-channel hydraulics to interconnected continental drainage systems. In cardiovascular modeling, researchers solved this by coupling 3D/1D models for major vessels with zero-dimensional Windkessel compartments for peripheral networks, using impedance-matching at bifurcations and vessel-wall compliance for distributed storage. Adapting this to hydrology, mainstem rivers (high Strahler order) are solved with full 1D Saint-Venant equations, mid-order tributaries use simplified diffusion-wave or kinematic-wave approximations, and low-order headwater streams are reduced to lumped hydrologic response units with characteristic lag-attenuation transfer functions. Floodplain storage is modeled as lateral compliance elements at each computational node, analogous to elastic arterial walls, absorbing and releasing flow during flood pulses. Junction conditions enforce mass and momentum conservation using impedance-based coupling that naturally handles backwater effects at confluences. The hierarchical decomposition is governed by stream order, with automatic model-fidelity assignment: the framework selects the appropriate level of physical detail based on each reach's position in the network topology, drainage area, and flood sensitivity. This approach enables continental-scale simulations (e.g., entire Mississippi or Amazon basins) at computational costs orders of magnitude lower than uniform high-fidelity discretization, while preserving the essential hydraulic dynamics—wave propagation, backwater, and floodplain exchange—that govern large-scale flood routing and water resource management.

ideas.title: Hybrid CLSVOF-AMR framework with neural-network interface correction for robust free-surface prediction
ideas.description: This work introduces a hybrid computational fluid dynamics framework that couples the level-set and volume-of-fluid methods (CLSVOF) with block-structured adaptive mesh refinement to predict unknown free-surface positions in multiphase flows with high fidelity and controlled computational cost. To overcome the residual numerical diffusion and curvature errors inherent even in coupled methods, a physics-informed neural network is trained offline on high-resolution benchmark solutions and deployed inline as a correction operator that refines interface position and surface tension estimates at each time step. The AMR strategy dynamically allocates resolution to the evolving free surface guided by both gradient-based refinement criteria and uncertainty estimates from the neural corrector, ensuring that computational effort scales with interface complexity rather than domain volume. The framework is validated on canonical free-surface problems including dam-break, sloshing, and wave-structure interaction, demonstrating superior mass conservation, sharper interface resolution, and order-of-magnitude speedups over uniformly refined VOF or level-set simulations alone.

ideas.title: Sediment transport through gabion stepped chutes

ideas.sourceTitle: Air–water flows down stepped chutes: turbulence and flow structure observations

ideas.title: Adapted Buckingham Pi multi-force similitude from spray combustion to aeration jet scaling
ideas.description: Dynamic similarity for aeration devices is challenging because both gravity (Froude number) and surface tension (Weber number) forces are simultaneously important, making classical single-parameter similitude insufficient. An analogous multi-force scaling problem exists in spray combustion engineering, where liquid fuel atomization in gas turbines depends concurrently on inertial, surface tension, and viscous forces. The atomization community resolved this by applying the Buckingham Pi theorem to derive composite dimensionless groups—most notably combining Weber and Ohnesorge numbers into generalized stability parameters that collapse multiple breakup regimes onto unified regime maps (Reitz & Bracco 1982; Lefebvre 1989). This allowed sub-scale nozzle experiments to reliably predict full-scale combustor spray behavior. Adapting this approach, we propose constructing composite aeration similarity parameters that combine Fr and We (and potentially Re) into reduced dimensionless groups tailored to aerator jet physics. By systematically varying jet velocity, nozzle geometry, and fluid properties across laboratory scales, one can map aeration regimes (incipient entrainment, developed bubble plume, jet atomization) onto a regime diagram parameterized by these composite groups. The resulting framework would identify dominant scaling parameters for each regime, establish regime transition boundaries, and ultimately provide practitioners with scale-independent design criteria for aerator jets—eliminating the current need to simultaneously and independently match multiple dimensionless numbers during physical model testing.

ideas.title: Adaptive boundary evolution for sediment transport inspired by free-boundary tumor growth models
ideas.description: Traditional sediment transport models treat the bed as a fixed or quasi-static boundary, analogous to how early tumor models assumed a static tissue domain. In mathematical oncology, this limitation was overcome by treating the tumor margin as a free boundary whose evolution is coupled to internal reaction-diffusion dynamics via level-set or phase-field methods. We adapt this approach to fluvial geomorphology by representing the sediment-water interface as a phase-field variable governed by an evolution equation driven by local bed shear stress, sediment flux divergence, and gravitational settling. The phase-field smoothly transitions between the fluid and sediment phases, eliminating the need for explicit mesh deformation while naturally handling topological changes such as scour holes merging or bedform splitting. The flow solver and bed evolution equation are coupled implicitly: at each time step the updated bed morphology alters the computational flow domain, producing new velocity and stress fields that feed back into the bed evolution. This framework enables simulation of complex emergent phenomena—ripple formation, dune migration, and channel avulsion—without prescribing bed geometry a priori, offering a principled alternative to empirical morphodynamic correction factors currently used in engineering practice.

ideas.title: Dual problem: sediment flux into stream

ideas.title: Hierarchical uncertainty decomposition for multi-scale hydraulic model validation using Bayesian error attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical Bayesian framework that decomposes the total discrepancy between hydraulic model predictions and field observations into distinct, quantifiable error sources: measurement noise, boundary condition uncertainty, parameter estimation error, and irreducible structural model inadequacy. The framework fuses data across multiple scales—from controlled laboratory flume experiments through instrumented prototypes to large-scale field deployments—using a multi-fidelity Gaussian process surrogate to transfer validated error structures across scales. By applying this methodology to both physical (scaled) and numerical (CFD/shallow-water) hydraulic models validated against benchmark field datasets from dam spillways, river confluences, and coastal structures, the research produces actionable diagnostics that identify precisely which model components require improvement rather than simply reporting aggregate goodness-of-fit metrics. The outcome is a validated, open-source diagnostic toolkit that transforms hydraulic model validation from binary pass/fail assessments into targeted, uncertainty-aware model improvement roadmaps.

ideas.title: Capillary-Engineered Open Microfluidic Channels via Surface Energy Gradient Design
ideas.description: This work develops a framework for open-surface microfluidic flow by replacing gravity-driven hydraulic principles with capillary pressure gradients generated through spatially patterned wettability on channel surfaces. Lithographically defined hydrophobic barriers confine liquid to hydrophilic tracks, creating stable free-surface flow without enclosure, while graded contact angle profiles along the flow direction provide the driving pressure differential analogous to bed slope in classical open channels. A modified shallow-water model incorporating Young-Laplace capillary pressure, Marangoni stresses, and contact angle hysteresis is derived and validated against experiments in silicon-glass hybrid chips with feature sizes from 10 to 500 micrometers. The approach enables open-access microfluidic platforms for cell culture, biosensing, and on-chip chemistry where pipette or probe access to the flowing liquid is required.

ideas.title: Actively Stabilized Microscale Segmented Flows via On-Chip Feedback Control
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop microfluidic platform for generating and maintaining precise, user-defined aerated flow patterns (e.g., monodisperse bubble trains, stable liquid plugs) where surface forces dominate. The system overcomes inherent instabilities by embedding real-time sensing of interfacial dynamics and employing localized actuators to correct deviations from the target flow regime. This enables reproducible, long-duration experiments and processes that are impossible with passive systems, unlocking robust applications in microscale chemical reactions, material synthesis, and biological assays requiring controlled gas-liquid interfaces.

ideas.title: Adapting pharmacokinetic neural network modeling to sediment transport prediction
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy between predicting sediment transport rates from channel flow parameters and predicting drug concentration dynamics from patient physiological parameters in pharmacokinetics. Both domains involve predicting the flux of material through a system with nonlinear, threshold-dependent dynamics and high variability across different conditions. In pharmacokinetics, hybrid neural network models that embed mechanistic compartmental equations into network architectures have achieved state-of-the-art prediction accuracy while maintaining physiological interpretability. We adapt this approach to sediment transport by designing a physics-informed neural network where known hydraulic and sediment mechanics relationships (critical shear stress thresholds, Einstein-Brown type transport functions, settling velocity laws) are encoded as structural constraints or sub-modules within the network. The network then learns site-specific corrections and interaction effects from measured data of channel geometry, flow velocity, grain size distribution, and observed sediment discharge. This hybrid approach is expected to outperform both classical empirical formulas and pure black-box ML models, particularly in data-scarce fluvial environments where generalization from limited field measurements is essential.

ideas.title: Bayesian multi-regime inversion of channel geometry with learned geometric priors and uncertainty quantification
ideas.description: This work develops a Bayesian inverse framework that reconstructs unknown river channel cross-section geometry from surface velocity and water level measurements collected across multiple flow regimes, exploiting the distinct geometric sensitivities of low, moderate, and high flows to resolve the fundamental non-uniqueness of single-condition inversion. A conditional normalizing flow trained on a large database of surveyed natural cross-sections serves as a data-driven geometric prior that regularizes the solution space toward physically plausible shapes, replacing ad hoc smoothness penalties. The forward model is a differentiable 1D shallow-water solver enabling gradient-based posterior sampling via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, producing full probability distributions over channel geometry that rigorously quantify reconstruction uncertainty. The framework is validated on synthetic channels with realistic noise levels and field-tested on gauged river reaches where independent bathymetric surveys provide ground truth, demonstrating that multi-regime observations combined with learned priors reduce geometric ambiguity by an order of magnitude compared to classical single-discharge inversion.

ideas.title: Stability-constrained dynamic bed morphology with data-assimilated feedback coupling
ideas.description: This approach replaces fixed-bed assumptions with a dynamically evolving bed morphology that feeds back into flow calculations, but critically embeds a multi-scale stabilization framework to prevent the numerical instabilities and runaway feedback that plague naive two-way coupling schemes. A physics-informed regularization layer enforces empirically derived constraints on maximum bed change rates and permissible bed-form geometries, ensuring that morphological updates remain within physically realistic bounds across spatial scales. Periodic data-assimilation windows incorporate observed bathymetric measurements to correct drift accumulation, enabling stable long-duration simulations that fixed-bed models cannot achieve. The system is validated against flume experiments with known bed evolution trajectories, directly testing whether the stabilized coupling reproduces transient bed forms more accurately than both static-bed and unconstrained dynamic-bed approaches.

ideas.title: Phase-field interface tracking adapted from materials science for free-surface prediction
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between free-surface prediction in computational fluid dynamics and solid-liquid interface tracking in computational materials science. In materials science, the phase-field method has become the standard for modeling solidification, where a diffuse order parameter replaces the sharp solid-liquid boundary and evolves according to a free-energy functional that encodes interfacial energy, curvature penalties, and bulk thermodynamics. This eliminates the need for explicit front tracking and gracefully handles complex topological events like dendritic branching and coalescence. Adapting this to CFD free-surface problems, we replace traditional volume-of-fluid or level-set formulations with a phase-field model where a continuous variable distinguishes liquid from gas phases. The interface evolves via a Cahn-Hilliard equation coupled to the Navier-Stokes equations, with surface tension emerging naturally from the free-energy gradient rather than being imposed as a singular force. This approach offers several advantages for predicting unknown free-surface positions: it provides a variational framework that guarantees energy consistency, it handles merging and breakup of free surfaces without special algorithms, and it can incorporate complex physics such as contact-angle hysteresis and surfactant effects through modifications to the free-energy functional. The method is particularly promising for problems where the free-surface topology is not known a priori, such as wave breaking, droplet impact, or mold filling in casting processes.

ideas.title: Bayesian sequential assimilation of bed form evolution for robust flow resistance estimation
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fundamental ill-posedness of inverting static bed forms by assimilating time-resolved sequences of bed topography into a Bayesian inference framework, where the dynamical evolution of bed forms provides far stronger constraints on flow resistance than any single snapshot. Physics-informed priors derived from established sediment transport scaling relations regularize the inverse problem and prevent spurious solutions, while the Bayesian formulation naturally propagates measurement uncertainty into posterior distributions over resistance coefficients. The method is validated using synthetic data with controlled noise levels to demonstrate convergence guarantees, then applied to flume experiments with known ground-truth resistance values. This sequential data assimilation strategy transforms an ill-posed static inversion into a well-constrained dynamical estimation problem with quantified uncertainty.

ideas.title: Electrical impedance tomography-inspired reconstruction of channel cross-section geometry from surface flow measurements
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy between inferring channel cross-section geometry from surface flow measurements and reconstructing internal tissue conductivity from boundary voltage measurements in electrical impedance tomography (EIT). Both are ill-posed inverse problems where sparse boundary/surface observations must be used to recover a spatially distributed internal property governed by a PDE-based forward model. In EIT, the community has developed mature inversion tools including adjoint-based sensitivity analysis, Gauss-Newton and Levenberg-Marquardt iterative solvers, and sophisticated regularization strategies (Tikhonov, total variation, sparsity-promoting priors) that handle ill-posedness while preserving sharp internal boundaries. We propose adapting this machinery to the open-channel flow setting: the Saint-Venant or Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes equations replace the elliptic conductivity equation as the forward model, surface velocity fields and water level time series replace boundary voltages as observations, and the unknown channel bed and wall geometry replaces the internal conductivity distribution. Total variation regularization is particularly well-suited here, as channel cross-sections often feature sharp transitions (e.g., bank edges, ledges) analogous to organ boundaries in medical imaging. The dual-scaling nature of the problem—coupling local cross-section shape with reach-scale hydraulic response—mirrors EIT's multi-scale sensitivity patterns and can be addressed through multi-resolution parameterization strategies developed in that field.

ideas.title: Physics-informed neural networks with uncertainty quantification for generalizable sediment transport prediction across diverse fluvial environments
ideas.description: This work develops a physics-informed neural network framework for sediment transport prediction that embeds classical transport equations as differentiable constraints within the network architecture, allowing the model to learn residual corrections rather than the full input-output mapping from scratch. By enforcing dimensional consistency and known scaling relationships (e.g., Shields parameter thresholds, power-law dependencies on shear stress), the model maintains physical plausibility even under distribution shift to unseen channel geometries and grain size distributions. Deep ensembles provide calibrated uncertainty estimates that flag unreliable predictions when field conditions diverge from training data, enabling safe deployment. The framework is validated on a multi-site dataset spanning laboratory flumes, gravel-bed rivers, and sand-bed channels to demonstrate superior generalization compared to both purely empirical formulas and unconstrained neural network baselines.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Multi-Scale Design of Hydraulic Structures using Learned Scale-Bias Corrections
ideas.description: This research fuses machine learning for scale effect correction with probabilistic hydraulic design to create a new framework for optimizing structures under climate uncertainty. A neural network is trained to learn and correct systematic biases between high-fidelity but scarce data (e.g., detailed prototypes or field measurements) and abundant low-fidelity data (e.g., scaled lab experiments or coarse numerical models). This corrected, unified physical model is then embedded within a probabilistic optimization routine that evaluates performance across ensembles of flood loads and future climate scenarios. The result is a design methodology that produces structures optimized for real-world performance and future risk, whose reliability is enhanced by explicitly mitigating the scale-induced inaccuracies inherent in traditional multi-scale analysis.

ideas.title: Microfluidic bubble logic using capillary waveguides
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from integrated photonics to solve the challenge of controlling aerated flows in microfluidic chips. In photonics, waveguides and resonators manipulate light signals on-chip. Analogously, we propose designing 'capillary waveguides'—microchannels with precisely engineered surface energy patterns or cross-sectional geometries—to confine, guide, and manipulate individual gas bubbles within a continuous liquid phase. Surface tension, dominating at this scale, acts like total internal reflection, directing bubbles along predetermined paths. This enables the creation of bubble-based logic gates (e.g., AND, OR gates at T-junctions), memory elements (trapped bubbles), and signal processing units, forming a foundational architecture for surface-tension-dominated microfluidic computation and complex multi-phase flow control.

ideas.title: Galaxy clustering statistics as a template for quantifying scale-dependent bubble clustering distortion
ideas.description: In observational cosmology, quantifying how galaxy clustering statistics depend on the scale of observation is a mature problem solved through n-point correlation functions, power spectral analysis, and rigorous finite-volume corrections. Galaxies form clusters, filaments, and voids whose statistical signatures must be compared across surveys of different volumes and resolutions—directly analogous to air bubbles forming clusters of varying morphology across differently scaled stepped spillway models. This analogy suggests importing the cosmological toolkit: (1) compute the two-point correlation function g(r) of bubble centroids in 3D or 2D cross-sections at each model scale, extracting the correlation length and power-law exponent; (2) apply window-function corrections that account for the finite flow domain and sensor geometry at each scale, removing measurement artifacts from the true clustering signal; (3) construct a scaling relation linking correlation parameters to geometric scale ratio, analogous to the cosmological scaling of r0 with survey depth. Additionally, the void probability function—widely used to characterize empty regions between galaxy clusters—can be adapted to quantify the probability of bubble-free zones at each scale, providing a complementary clustering metric. This framework would move beyond simple bulk statistics (mean void fraction, mean bubble size) to a full spatial-statistical description of how bubble clustering distorts with scale, enabling researchers to establish correction factors for translating model-scale clustering data to prototype conditions.
